Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
July 18, 2022 
SUBJECT:
High Voltage Streetlight Conversion (Phase 4) Design Services – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with HDR Engineering, Inc. for design services for High Voltage Streetlight Conversion (Phase 4) in 
an amount not to exceed $259,400; establish a contingency reserve of $25,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to 
execute the agreement in substantially the form presented and approve amendments within the contingency amount. 
BACKGROUND:
The High Voltage Streetlight Conversion Project will replace all high voltage streetlight circuits with new low voltage 
circuits to minimize streetlight outages, improve circuit reliability, allow for conversion to LED lighting, and reduce 
maintenance needs.  The project is phased to design and construct the replacement of circuits as funding becomes 
available.  Phase 4 will convert nine (9) circuits and will complete the citywide conversion project.
Staff solicited a proposal from HDR Engineering, Inc. (HDR) to complete the High Voltage Streetlight Conversion (Phase 4) 
design. HDR recently acquired WRECO, Inc., one of the consultants selected for the City’s list of on-call consultants to 
complete engineering services. HDR also recently completed engineering design services for the High Voltage Streetlight 
Conversion (Phase 3) Project, and staff is satisfied with the services provided for that project. Staff recommends entering 
an agreement with HDR to perform the design services for this project.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds are available in the High Voltage Streetlight Conversion Project (No. 460040) to award this agreement and 
fund the contingency reserve. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as a “replacement or reconstruction,” because it consists of the 
replacement or reconstruction of existing utility systems and/or facilities that will be located on the same site as the 
structure replaced for substantially the same purpose and involving negligible or no expansion of capacity. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15302(c).)  Additionally, the project is exempt as “minor alterations to land,” because it includes minor 
trenching and backfilling where the surface is restored. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15304(f)).
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Att 1 – Agreement

SECTION 19 - MEDIATION 
 
 
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw. 
 
 
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

Scope of Services 
The HDR|WRECO Team’s proposed scope of services includes designing and preparing the construction 
contract documents for the City of San Mateo’s (City) High Voltage Streetlight Conversion Project ‐ Phase 
4 (Project). The following Regulated Output (RO) Circuits and limits are included in this scope of services.   
 
 
RO Circuits
# of Streetlight Replacement 
1 
Dartmouth Road 
13 
2 
Baywood Avenue 
4 
3 
Garfield Street 
13 
4 
El Cerrito Avenue 
7 
5 
20th Avenue 
13 
6 
31st Avenue 
16 
7 
Claremont Street 
17 
8 
41st Avenue 
19 
9 
Tilia Street 
16 
     
The  Project  is  part  of  an  ongoing  effort  by  the  City  to  convert  its  remaining  outdated  high  voltage 
streetlight  circuits  to  modern  low  voltage  circuits.  The  Project  will  consist  of  preparing  the  plans, 
specifications, and estimate (PS&E) for the conversion of the nine street locations.  Existing poles and pull 
boxes will remain and be reused if they are in good condition. New service conductors and conduits will 
be installed, preferably by trenchless methods. Existing conduits and conductors will be abandoned in 
place. Existing fixtures will be replaced by new LED fixtures.  Existing circuits may be divided or combined 
as needed to achieve a good design that balances construction cost, ease of maintenance, and minimizing 
the number of service connections. 
 
TASK 1 – PROJECT MANAGEMENT AND COORDINATION 
Task 1.1 ‐ Project Schedule 
Upon receiving the Notice‐to‐Proceed (NTP), HDR|WRECO will prepare a preliminary Project schedule for 
the City’s review. The schedule will identify the critical path items, review times, and milestone submittals. 
The schedule will be submitted to the City within five (5) working days of receiving the NTP.  HDR|WRECO 
will update and issue the revised Project schedule as necessary. 
 
Task 1.2 ‐ Project Kickoff Meeting (Virtual) 
Upon receiving the NTP, HDR|WRECO will coordinate a Project Kickoff Meeting with the City.  The purpose 
of the meeting will be to gain approval of the proposed base schedule and to identify any known Project 
issues.  Meeting minutes will be prepared and distributed within five (5) working days of the meeting. 
 
Task 1.3 – Bi‐Weekly Progress Meetings (Virtual) 
The  HDR|WRECO  Team  will  attend  up  to  twenty‐four  (24)  virtual  meetings  with  the  City.  Progress 
meetings will occur every two weeks during design with at least one meeting during each stage of design 
(35%, 50%, 65%, 95%, and 100%).  Agendas for progress meetings shall include, at a minimum, schedule 
updates, discussion of critical path items, design coordination and resolving any issues that arise during 
the design process. 
 
151 of 615

--- page 152 ---
1000 Broadway, Suite 475 
Oakland, CA 94607 
Phone:  510.836.5188 
www.wreco.com 
 
 
 
                         | Civil Engineering | Environmental Compliance | Geotechnical Engineering | Water Resources |     4 
Task 1.4 ‐ Project Review Meetings (Virtual) 
The HDR|WRECO Team will attend up to four (4) review meetings with the City. The primary purpose of 
each meeting will be to walk City staff through the design plans and bring design issues to the City’s 
attention.  Meeting will be held after each submittal (35%, 65%, 95% and 100%).  HDR|WRECO will provide 
meeting minutes to the City and team members.  These meetings should typically be scheduled to occur 
within one week after the submittal is made. 
 
Task 1.5 – On‐Site Field Meetings (2 total) 
The HDR|WRECO Team will attend one on‐site pre‐design meeting to exchange information between City 
O&M staff and the Consultant’s team.  This meeting should be considered separate from the Consultant’s 
site  walk.    The  Team  will  attend  another  on‐site  follow‐up  meeting  to  provide  an  opportunity  for 
additional on‐site time if any is needed to address issues that arise during design. 
 
Task 1.6 ‐ Quality Control 
The HDR|WRECO Team’s designated Quality Assurance/Quality Control (QA/QC) Manager will ensure the 
QA/QC procedures established for the Project will be adhered to by all HDR|WRECO Team members. The 
following QC procedures will be used in the development of the planning and engineering documents for 
the Project: 
 
Define key team members and their respective roles and responsibilities for the Project. 
 
Identify, categorize, and incorporate correspondence, memoranda, and reports prepared for the 
Project within a distinct filing system that enables quick recovery upon request by the City and/or 
HDR|WRECO Team members. 
 
Perform internal plan QC reviews by the Project Manager and Team Leaders to ensure conflicts 
and miscommunication do not occur. 
 
Perform QA reviews by senior management who are not involved with the development of the 
Project documents at the 35%, 50%, 65%, 95%, and 100% PS&E milestones before submittal to 
the City. 
 
Task 1 Deliverables 
 
Project Schedule 
 
Project Meeting Invites 
 
Project Meeting Agendas 
 
Project Meeting Minutes 
 
Monthly Invoices and Progress Reports 
 
 
TASK 2 – PRE‐DESIGN INVESTIGATIONS 
Task 2.1 ‐ Field Reconnaissance & Investigation 
The HDR|WRECO Team will conduct a site walk to perform simple visual topographic survey to confirm 
location of streetlights, pull boxes, ROs, and other features, such as fire hydrants, street trees, utility poles, 
and other utility boxes or vaults, in the vicinity of work to be done. The Team will also identify unusual 
conditions that may require additional work by the contractor in order to perform the conversion, such 
as pole bases or pull boxes that are in bushes or pavers. 
 
 
152 of 615

--- page 153 ---
1000 Broadway, Suite 475 
Oakland, CA 94607 
Phone:  510.836.5188 
www.wreco.com 
 
 
 
                         | Civil Engineering | Environmental Compliance | Geotechnical Engineering | Water Resources |     5 
Task 2.2 – Review City Data and Partial Design 
The HDR|WRECO Team will review the existing circuit maps with City staff to verify remaining conversion 
work to be done.  The Team understands that a portions of the Dartmouth, Baywood, and El Cerrito 
circuits have been partially designed and the conduit installed, or the conduit will be installed in the near 
future, as part of roadway maintenance projects.  These partial  designs will  be incorporated into our 
design.   
 
Task 2.3 – Right of Way Research 
The HDR|WRECO Team will conduct right‐of‐way research to establish extent of public right of way and 
availability of utility easements. This will include review of right‐of‐way maps provided by the City and 
accessor maps but is not intended to include any surveying work. 
 
Task 2.4 ‐ Utility Investigations & Potholing 
The HDR|WRECO Team will conduct a search of potential utility owners who might have facilities within 
the limits of the Project on each street. We will prepare utility request letters for the City to send to the 
owners requesting record drawings of their facilities or confirming they do not have any facilities that 
could be in conflict with the proposed improvements. 
 
A composite plan of existing utilities will be prepared based on the information received from the utility 
owners and utility information received from the City. Potential conflicts between existing utilities and 
the proposed street light improvements will be identified. 
 
The HDR|WRECO Team will prepare a potholing plan and discuss the proposed plan with the City.  After 
reaching an agreement on the potholing plan, HDR|WRECO and its potholing contractor will positively 
locate utilities with an allowance  budget not to exceed $25,000. It  is assumed traffic  control will be 
required.  
 
Task 2.5‐ Develop Design Base Maps 
The HDR|WRECO Team will prepare the design base maps for each street in AutoCAD utilizing aerial maps. 
 
Task 2.6 ‐ 35% Conceptual Plans 
The 35% design plans prepared under this task will include the following: 
 
Preparing schematic circuit maps showing extents of each individual low voltage circuit on one 
sheet.  Schematic  circuit  map  shall  show  location  of  lights,  pull  boxes,  conduits,  and  service 
connection point. Maps shall include streetlight ID numbers, pole types, fixture types, address of 
the service connection point, and total circuit load. Schematic circuit maps shall be suitable for 
attachment to PG&E service applications.  Schematic circuit maps will be formatted to fit 11” x 
17” sheets. 
 
Task 2.7 – Prepare PG&E Load Calcs and Voltage Drop Calcs 
The WRECO Team will prepare the PG&E load calculations worksheet for each low voltage circuit and the 
voltage drop calculations. 
 
Task 2 Deliverables 
 
Composite Plan of Existing Utilities (PDF)

35% Conceptual Plans (PDF) 
 
PG&E Load Calcs and Voltage Drop Calcs (PDF) 
 
TASK 3 – 50% DESIGN SUBMITTAL 
Task 3.1 – 50% Plans 
The  50%  submittal  shall  consist  of  documents  required  for  the  PG&E  service  applications,  including 
schematic circuit maps and load calculation worksheets. The 50% submittal shall be made after the Team 
and City staff have completed addressing all circuit layout related comments on the 35% plans and there 
is a high level of confidence that the layouts are in final form. 
 
Task 3 Deliverables 
 
50% Design Plans (PDF) 
 
TASK 4 – 65% DESIGN SUBMITTAL 
 
Task 4.1 ‐ 65% Plans 
The 65% design plans prepared under this task will include the following: 
 
Title Sheet with Vicinity Map and General Notes, 
 
Index Map Sheet, 
 
Electrical Plans, and 
 
Construction Details (including preparing two (2) details for raising existing streetlight bases to 
finish grade) 
 
All sheets will be prepared on the City’s Standard Title Block and in AutoCAD (Version 2018). 
 
Task 4.2 ‐ 65% Specifications 
Specifications will be written in the format of the City’s standard contract book and Caltrans’ Standard 
Plans and Specifications, dated 2018.  The City will provide the standard “front‐end” specifications for the 
HDR|WRECO Team’s use. We will review the Boilerplate Front End specifications and recommend any 
appropriate changes. 
 
Task 4.3 ‐ 65% Construction Cost Estimate 
The HDR|WRECO Team will prepare a construction cost estimate to ensure the proposed improvements 
are within the available construction budget. The construction cost estimate will be based on historical 
unit costs obtained from recent projects near the Project area, as well as Caltrans’ construction cost data. 
 
Task 4 Deliverables 
 
65% Plans (PDF) 
 
65% Technical Specifications (Word) 
 
65% Construction Cost Estimate (Excel) 
 
 
 
 
154 of 615

--- page 155 ---
1000 Broadway, Suite 475 
Oakland, CA 94607 
Phone:  510.836.5188 
www.wreco.com 
 
 
 
                         | Civil Engineering | Environmental Compliance | Geotechnical Engineering | Water Resources |     7 
TASK 5 – 95% DESIGN SUBMITTAL 
Task 5.1 ‐ Response to 65% Comments 
Upon  receiving  comments  on  the  65%  submittal,  the  HDR|WRECO  Team  will  prepare  a  Comment 
Response  Form  (CRF)  to  document  the  City’s  comments.  We  will  prepare  written  responses  to  each 
comment and submit the form to the City prior to the Project Review Meeting. After discussion with the 
City and upon reaching an agreement, the CRF will be updated to document the final status of each 
comment. 
 
Task 5.2 ‐ 95% Plans 
Upon reaching an agreement with  the City on the status of  the 65% PS&E  submittal  comments, the 
HDR|WRECO Team will revise the plans accordingly to prepare the 95% plans. 
 
Task 5.3 ‐ 95% Specifications 
The HDR|WRECO Team will update the technical specifications based on the 65% submittal comments 
and the 95% plans. 
 
Task 5.4 ‐ 95% Construction Cost Estimate 
The HDR|WRECO Team will update the Construction Cost Estimate to reflect the 95% plans and 
specifications. 
 
Task 5 Deliverables 
 
65% CRF 
 
95% Plans (PDF) 
 
95% Technical Specifications (Word) 
 
95% Construction Cost Estimate (Excel) 
 
TASK 6 – 100% DESIGN SUBMITTAL 
Task 6.1 ‐ Response to 95% Comments 
Upon receiving comments on the 95% submittal, the HDR|WRECO Team will prepare a CRF to document 
the City’s comments. We will prepare written responses to each comment and submit the CRF to the City 
prior to the Project Review Meeting. After discussion with the City and upon reaching an agreement, the 
CRF will be updated to document the final status of each comment. 
 
Task 6.2 ‐ 100% Plans 
Upon reaching an agreement with  the City on the status of  the 95% PS&E  submittal  comments, the 
HDR|WRECO Team will revise the plans accordingly to prepare the 100% plans. 
 
Task 6.3 ‐ 100% Specifications 
The HDR|WRECO Team will update the technical specifications based on the 95% submittal comments 
and the 100% plans. 
 
Task 6.4 ‐ 100% Construction Cost Estimate 
The  HDR|WRECO  Team  will  update  the  Construction  Cost  Estimate  to  reflect  the  100%  plans  and 
specifications.



 
Final Construction Cost Estimate (Excel and PDF) 
 
TASK 7 – BIDDING AND CONSTRUCTION SUPPORT 
For this task, the HDR|WRECO Team will provide nominal, supplemental support to the City for bidding 
and construction inquiries.  
 
Task 7.1 – Bid Support 
We will perform the following: 
 
Attend the pre‐bid meeting, and  
 
Prepare drafts of addenda or clarifications. 
 
Task 7.2 – Construction Support 
We will perform the following: 
 
Attend the pre‐construction conference to respond to questions concerning the PS&E; 
 
Review and prepare responses to Requests for Information (RFI) from the contractor; 
 
Review the contractor’s submittals and shop drawings for compatibility with design; 
 
Assist the City in preparing the Contract Change Orders (CCO), if needed; 
 
Participate in the final walk‐through of the constructed Project;  
 
Perform  final  review/evaluation  and  assist  with  the  preparation  of  the  punch  list  of  work 
deficiencies; and 
 
Preparing a shape file or AutoCAD file of the streetlight circuits suitable for import into the City’s 
ArcGIS streetlight layer.  Data to be imported shall include approximate geographic locations of 
the  service  connections,  pull  boxes,  conduits,  and  poles.  Data  for  the  poles  shall  include  ID 
numbers, pole types, fixture types, and fixture wattages. The City’s GIS is based on the State Plane 
NAD83  CA  Zone  III  coordinate  system.  The  final  GIS  import  file  shall  be  consistent  with  the 
contractor’s final red lined submittal at the end of construction. 
 
Task 7 Deliverables 
 
Contract Addenda (as‐needed) 
 
Answers to Bidders’ Questions (for distribution by the City during the bid phase) 
 
Responses to RFIs and CCOs 
 
Recommendations from the Final Review 
 
GIS Shape Import File 
 
 
TASK 8 – MISCELLANEOUS ENGINEERING SERVICES ALLOWANCE 
Expenditure of this allowance will be authorized by the City on a task‐by‐task basis using task orders as 
negotiated prices.  This allowance shall not exceed $10,000.

                         | Civil Engineering | Environmental Compliance | Geotechnical Engineering | Water Resources |     9 
Project Assumptions 
The HDR|WRECO Team’s proposed scope of services and budget are provided based on the following 
assumptions. 
 
The City will: 
 
Provide environmental clearance for the Project; 
 
Prepare and submit the PG&E service application; 
 
Provide construction inspection work and contract administration for the Project; 
 
Provide the HDR|WRECO Team with any City as‐built or record drawings, utility underground 
maps, and any existing information necessary to complete the design; 
 
Provide the front end “boilerplate” for the specifications to the HDR|WRECO Team; and 
 
Be responsible for the reproduction of the construction bid packages (plans and specifications). 
 
The HDR|WRECO Team also assumes the following: 
 
No right‐of‐way mapping will be required; 
 
No major regulatory permits are needed; 
 
Construction work will be performed exclusively in the existing right‐of‐way; and 
 
An Encroachment Permit (if needed) will be provided at no cost to HDR|WRECO. 
 
The Basis of Design assumption includes the following: 
 
Existing poles will be reused. 
 
Fixtures will be replaced by LED fixtures from the City’s current list of standard fixtures. 
 
Selection of LED fixtures will be based on City’s standard usage chart. Consultant may recommend 
variations from the standard usage based on existing lighting conditions and roadway conditions. 
 
New circuits shall typically be limited to a maximum of 12 lights. 
 
New circuits shall typically be limited to a maximum of 1,200 feet from service point to farthest 
light. 
 
New service connections shall be established at existing PG&E secondary locations if possible. If 
no suitable secondary locations are available consult with the City to determine if connecting to 
nearby existing low voltage circuits, exceptions to the limits on number of lights on a circuit, or 
exceptions to the limits on length of the circuit will be allowed. 
 
New  circuit  operating  voltage  shall  be  240V  and  lights  connected  in  standard  parallel 
configuration. 
 
Circuit design shall be in accordance with City’s streetlighting details. 
 
Typically, new conduits and conductors shall be installed for the low voltage circuits. As noted 
previously, some exceptions exist where partial installations of conduit have, or will, occur as part 
of other projects. The City will provide information about the location of existing conduit at the 
project kick‐off meeting. 
 
Existing pull boxes shall typically be reused except as follows: 
o Pull boxes located in a pedestrian curb ramp or ramp flare shall be relocated out of the 
ramp or flare. 
o City  O&M  staff  will  provide  additional  direction  regarding  pull  boxes  they  would  like 
replaced or relocated due to poor condition, poor location, or for other reasons. 
157 of 615

--- page 158 ---
1000 Broadway, Suite 475 
Oakland, CA 94607 
Phone:  510.836.5188 
www.wreco.com 
 
 
 
                         | Civil Engineering | Environmental Compliance | Geotechnical Engineering | Water Resources |     10 
o Based on site inspection the HDR|WRECO Team may also recommend replacement or 
relocation of existing pull boxes due to poor condition, poor location, or failure to comply 
with current City design standards. 
 
New pull boxes shall be added as needed to comply with current City design standards. 
 
Streetlight  bases  that  are  below  finished  sidewalk  grade  shall  be  raised  to  finished  grade  if 
conversion work requires demolition of the sidewalk around the base. 
 
All PS&E shall be organized such that the individual circuits may be converted in phases which are 
to be determined at a later date. 
 
Work Not Included in Scope of Services 
The following tasks are not included in our proposed scope of services because they are not specifically 
noted from the City’s request. However, the HDR|WRECO Team can provide these services upon the City’s 
request and approval.  
 
Coordination with local businesses, schools, and residents impacted by the Project. 
 
Development of the Stormwater Pollution Prevention Plan. 
 
Development of the Traffic Handling Plans and Stage Construction, which will be the contractor’s 
responsibility.

EXHIBIT C 
 
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS 
 
 
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE  
Coverage shall be at least as broad as: 
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate limit 
applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or the 
general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit. 
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if CONSULTANT 
has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no less than 
$1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage. 
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury 
or disease.  
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s 
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate 
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and shall 
be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT. 
Other Insurance Provisions 
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions: 
Additional Insured Status 
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used). 
Primary Coverage 
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it. 
Notice of Cancellation 
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City. 
161 of 615

--- page 162 ---
Design Professional Services 2020 
 
Page 10 of 10 
Rev 7/21 
Waiver of Subrogation 
CONSULTANT hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said 
CONSULTANT may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  
CONSULTANT agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of 
subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of 
subrogation endorsement from the insurer.   
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions 
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the CONSULTANT to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof 
of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within 
the retention.   
Acceptability of Insurers 
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City. 
Verification of Coverage 
CONSULTANT shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies 
of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the CONSULTANT’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  11
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDA Acct No.: 10-4662
File ID: 22-6349
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
July 18, 2022 
SUBJECT:
Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 3 – Agreements and Budget Transfer
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to authorize a budget transfer from Citywide Street Reconstruction Package 2 to Package 1 in the 
amount of $2,900,000; approve an agreement with Teichert Construction for the Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 3 
Project in the amount of $4,229,667 and establish a contingency reserve of $423,000; approve an agreement with 
Ghirardelli Associates for construction inspection support services in the amount of $150,000 and establish a contingency 
reserve of $15,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the agreements in substantially the form 
presented and issue change orders within the contingency amounts.
BACKGROUND:
The Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 3 Project (Project) is part of the Public Works Smooth Streets Program 
(Program). The Project will repair approximately 1.6 miles of streets across nine locations with an Engineer’s Estimate for 
the project of $4,700,000.  Project locations are shown in the attached Location Map.
Due to the complex nature of the remaining street segments of the Program, in August 2021 the City issued a Request for 
Qualifications to pre-qualify contractors for the remaining phases of the Program. In order to be on the pre-qualification 
list, contractors needed to show that they have experience with soft/compressible soils and bay mud, subgrade 
stabilization methods, ability to place significant asphalt tonnage in one project, and experience working on residential 
roads. They also needed to certify that they have the typical contracting requirements for a public works project and a 
good safety record. During this process, the City pre-qualified four (4) contractors. Implementing a pre-qualification list for 
the remaining phases of the Program ensures the contractor that is awarded a particular phase of the Program has the 
experience and capability to execute the work efficiently with minimal impacts to the community.  
Agreement with Teichert Construction
The project was advertised on May 5, 2022, and a total of two (2) bids were received and opened on June 9, 2022. The bids 
received are listed below in order of increasing bid amount.
Company Name
Base Bid Amount
Additive Bid Item A Amount
Total Bid Amount
McGuire & Hester
$3,960,827.00
$57,980.00
$4,018,807.00
Teichert Construction
$4,201,417.00
$28,250.00
$4,229,667.00
Bids were compared on the basis of the total bid amount, which includes the Base Bid plus Additive Bid Item A. Additive 
Bid Item A includes costs to improve four curb ramps on Sunnybrae Boulevard that do not meet current Americans with 
163 of 615

--- page 164 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Disabilities Act (ADA) standards and are not required to be upgraded as part of the project since they are outside the 
paving limits, but will need to be upgraded at some point in the future. 
After review of the bids, staff found the apparent low bidder (McGuire & Hester) to be unresponsive as they did not list 
multiple subcontractors in their required list of subcontractors. Staff then determined that Teichert Construction is the 
lowest responsive, responsible bidder. Staff recommends City Council award the agreement to Teichert Construction, 
inclusive of the Additive Bid Item A scope based on Teichert’s pricing.
Agreement with Ghiradelli Associates, Inc. 
Public Works does not have sufficient staff resources to inspect and oversee construction activities. In May 2020, the City 
issued a Request for Proposals for on-call construction management services including inspection, general construction 
management, and specialty support services. Proposals were evaluated and three on-call construction management firms 
were selected: CSG Consultants, Inc., Ghirardelli Associates, Inc., and MNS Engineers, Inc.
Staff requested a proposal from Ghirardelli Associates for inspection services on this project. Staff determined Ghirardelli’s 
scope and budget are appropriate for the requested support services, and based on prior favorable experience working 
with Ghirardelli Associates, staff recommends awarding the construction inspection agreement to Ghirardelli Associates, 
Inc. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
These contracts were originally intended to be funded from the construction phase account, Citywide Street 
Reconstruction Package 2 (#467772); however, the existing contract documents (plans, specifications, and agreement) 
currently reference the design phase account, Citywide Street Reconstruction Package 1 (#467771). In order to minimize 
costs and avoid potential accounting inconsistencies between the design and construction phase project accounts, staff 
recommends leaving the contract documents unchanged and funding the proposed contracts from the design phase 
project account.  
Currently, there is an estimated available project account balance of $2,290,000 in the Citywide Street Reconstruction 
Package 1 (#467771), which is insufficient to award these contracts. The proposed resolution would authorize transfer of 
$2,900,000 from Citywide Street Reconstruction Package 2 (#467772), to Package 1 (#467771), providing sufficient funds 
to award these contracts. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This project is categorically exempt from CEQA as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Legal Work Day - Penalties for Violation. Eight hours of labor shall constitute a legal day's work.
Contractor shall not require more than 8 hours labor in a day and 40 hours in a calendar week
from any person employed by Contractor in the performance of such work unless such excess
work is compensated for at not less than 1-1/2 times the basic rate of pay. Contractor shall forfeit
as a penalty to City the sum of $25.00 for each laborer, workman or mechanic employed in the
execution of this contract by Contractor, or by any subcontractor for each calendar day during
which such laborer, workman or mechanic is required or permitted to labor more than 8 hours in
any calendar day and 40 hours in any one calendar week in violation of the provisions of Sections
1810 and 1816, inclusive, of the Labor Code of the State of California.

service under said subcontract or other arrangement at least the general prevailing rate of wages,

workers’ compensation or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer as defined by the 
Insurance Code. Contractor will defend City of San Mateo, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents, against any such claims. 
 
11. Attorney Fees. Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5,000 shall be recoverable as costs 
(that is, by the filing of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the 
provisions of the contract documents. The above $5,000 limit is the total of attorney fees 
recoverable whether in the trial court, appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number 
of attorneys, trials, appeals or actions. It is the intent that neither party to this Agreement shall have 
to pay the other more than $5,000 for attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions, to enforce 
the provisions of the contract documents. The parties expect and hope there will be no litigation and 
that any differences will be resolved amicably. 
 
12. Mediation. Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be 
submitted to mediation. The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request. The 
mediator shall be agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties 
shall each submit one name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, 
the California State Board of Mediation and Conciliation, or other agreed-upon service. The 
mediator shall be selected by a blind draw. 
 
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties. Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party. No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement. The mediation process, once

provisions that are valid under the applicable law and come closest to their original intention.

Exhibit A  
Insurance Requirements for Construction Contracts 
Contractor shall procure and maintain for the duration of the contract, and for x years thereafter, 
insurance against claims for injuries to persons or damages to property which may arise from or in 
connection with the performance of the work hereunder by the Contractor, his agents, 
representatives, employees, or subcontractors.  
MINIMUM SCOPE AND LIMIT OF INSURANCE  
Coverage shall be at least as broad as:  
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 covering CGL 
on an “occurrence” basis, including products and completed operations, property damage, bodily 
injury and personal & advertising injury with limits no less than $5,000,000 per occurrence. If a 
general aggregate limit applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this 
project/location (ISO CG 25 03 or 25 04) or the general aggregate limit shall be twice the required 
occurrence limit.   
2. Automobile Liability:  Insurance Services Office Form CA 0001 covering Code 1 (any auto), with 
limits no less than $5,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.  
3. Workers’ Compensation insurance as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, 
and Employers’ Liability insurance with a limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily 
injury or disease.  
4. Builder’s Risk (Course of Construction) insurance utilizing an “All Risk” (Special Perils) coverage 
form, with limits equal to the completed value of the project and no coinsurance penalty 
provisions. 
5. Surety Bonds as described below. 
6. Professional Liability (if Design/Build), with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence or 
claim, and $2,000,000 policy aggregate. 
7. Contractors’ Pollution Legal Liability and/or Asbestos Legal Liability and/or Errors and Omissions 
(if project involves environmental hazards) with limits no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or 
claim, and $2,000,000 policy aggregate. 
If the contractor maintains broader coverage and/or higher limits than the minimums shown above, 
the Entity requires and shall be entitled to the broader coverage and/or higher limits maintained by 
the contractor. Any available insurance proceeds in excess of the specified minimum limits of insurance 
and coverage shall be available to the Entity. 
Self-Insured Retentions  
Self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the Entity. At the option of the Entity, 
either: the contractor shall cause the insurer shall to reduce or eliminate such self-insured retentions 
as respects the Entity, its officers, officials, employees, and volunteers; or the Contractor shall provide 
a financial guarantee satisfactory to the Entity guaranteeing payment of losses and related 
investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses. The policy language shall provide, or be 
endorsed to provide, that the self-insured retention may be satisfied by either the named insured or 
Entity. 
 
Other Insurance Provisions  
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions: 
1. The Entity, its officers, officials, employees, and volunteers are to be covered as additional 
insureds on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations performed 
by or on behalf of the Contractor including materials, parts, or equipment furnished in connection 
173 of 615

--- page 174 ---
Construction Contract 
 
Page 8 of 9 
Rev 7/21 
with such work or operations and automobiles owned, leased, hired, or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the Contractor. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an endorsement to 
the Contractor’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, CG 11 85 or both CG 20 10, 
CG 20 26, CG 20 33, or CG 20 38; and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used). 
2. For any claims related to this project, the Contractor’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance coverage at least as broad as ISO CG 20 01 04 13 as respects the Entity, its officers, 
officials, employees, and volunteers. Any insurance or self-insurance maintained by the Entity, 
its officers, officials, employees, or volunteers shall be excess of the Contractor’s insurance and 
shall not contribute with it.  
3. Each insurance policy required by this clause shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, 
except with notice to the Entity. 
Builder’s Risk (Course of Construction) Insurance  
Contractor may submit evidence of Builder’s Risk insurance in the form of Course of Construction 
coverage. Such coverage shall name the Entity as a loss payee as their interest may appear. 
If the project does not involve new or major reconstruction, at the option of the Entity, an Installation 
Floater may be acceptable. For such projects, a Property Installation Floater shall be obtained that 
provides for the improvement, remodel, modification, alteration, conversion or adjustment to existing 
buildings, structures, processes, machinery and equipment.  The Property Installation Floater shall 
provide property damage coverage for any building, structure, machinery or equipment damaged, 
impaired, broken, or destroyed during the performance of the Work, including during transit, 
installation, and testing at the Entity’s site. 
Claims Made Policies – (If at all possible avoid and require occurrence type CGL policies) 
If any coverage required is written on a claims-made coverage form:  
1. The retroactive date must be shown, and this date must be before the execution date of the 
contract or the beginning of contract work. 
2. Insurance must be maintained and evidence of insurance must be provided for at least five (5) 
years after completion of contract work.  
3. If coverage is canceled or non-renewed, and not replaced with another claims-made policy form 
with a retroactive date prior to the contract effective, or start of work date, the Contractor must 
purchase extended reporting period coverage for a minimum of five (5) years after completion 
of contract work.  
4. A copy of the claims reporting requirements must be submitted to the Entity for review.  
5. If the services involve lead-based paint or asbestos identification/remediation, the Contractors 
Pollution Liability policy shall not contain lead-based paint or asbestos exclusions. If the services 
involve mold identification/remediation, the Contractors Pollution Liability policy shall not 
contain a mold exclusion, and the definition of Pollution shall include microbial matter, including 
mold.  
Acceptability of Insurers  
Insurance is to be placed with insurers authorized to conduct business in the state with a current A.M. 
Best rating of no less than A: VII, unless otherwise acceptable to the Entity.  
Waiver of Subrogation  
Contractor hereby agrees to waive rights of subrogation which any insurer of Contractor may acquire 
from Contractor by virtue of the payment of any loss. Contractor agrees to obtain any endorsement 
that may be necessary to affect this waiver of subrogation. The Workers’ Compensation policy shall be 
endorsed with a waiver of subrogation in favor of the Entity for all work performed by the Contractor, 
its employees, agents and subcontractors.

Verification of Coverage  
Contractor shall furnish the Entity with original Certificates of Insurance including all required 
amendatory endorsements (or copies of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by 
this clause) and a copy of the Declarations and Endorsement Page of the CGL policy listing all policy 
endorsements to Entity before work begins. However, failure to obtain the required documents prior to 
the work beginning shall not waive the Contractor’s obligation to provide them.  The Entity reserves the 
right to require complete, certified copies of all required insurance policies, including endorsements, 
required by these specifications, at any time.    
Subcontractors  
Contractor shall require and verify that all subcontractors maintain insurance meeting all the 
requirements stated herein, and Contractor shall ensure that Entity is an additional insured on insurance 
required from subcontractors. For CGL coverage subcontractors shall provide coverage with a form at 
least as broad as CG 20 38 04 13. 
Surety Bonds  
Contractor shall provide the following Surety Bonds: 
1. Bid bond 
2. Performance bond 
3. Payment bond 
4. Maintenance bond 
The Payment Bond and the Performance Bond shall be in a sum equal to the contract price. If the 
Performance Bond provides for a one-year warranty a separate Maintenance Bond is not necessary.  If 
the warranty period specified in the contract is for longer than one year a Maintenance Bond equal to 
10% of the contract price is required.  Bonds shall be duly executed by a responsible corporate surety, 
authorized to issue such bonds in the State of California and secured through an authorized agent with 
an office in California. 
Special Risks or Circumstances 
Entity reserves the right to modify these requirements, including limits, based on the nature of the risk, 
prior experience, insurer, coverage, or other circumstances.

This Agreement, made and entered into this day of ____________________, by and between the 
CITY OF SAN MATEO, a municipal corporation existing under the laws of the State of California (“CITY”), 
and Ghirardelli Associates, Inc., a corporation, (“CONSULTANT”), whose address is 1970 Broadway, Suite 
1220, Oakland, CA 94612. 
RECITALS: 
A. CITY desires certain professional inspection services hereinafter described.
B. CITY desires to engage CONSULTANT to provide these professional inspection services
by reason of its qualifications and experience for performing such services and CONSULTANT has offered 
to provide the required services on the terms and in the manner set forth herein. 
NOW, THEREFORE, IT IS AGREED as follows: 
SECTION 1 - SCOPE OF SERVICES 
The scope of services to be performed by CONSULTANT under this Agreement is as described in 
Exhibit A to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by reference. 
SECTION 2 - DUTIES OF CONSULTANT 
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for the professional quality, technical accuracy and 
coordination of all work furnished by CONSULTANT under this Agreement.  CONSULTANT shall, without 
additional compensation, correct or revise any errors or deficiencies in its work. 
CONSULTANT represents that it is qualified to furnish the services described under this 
Agreement. 
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for employing or engaging all persons necessary to perform 
the services of CONSULTANT. 
CONSULTANT agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage ordinance as codified in Municipal 
Code Chapter 5.92, which differs from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer Price Index 
escalator. 
SECTION 3 - DUTIES OF CITY 
CITY shall provide pertinent information regarding its requirements for the project. 
CITY shall examine documents submitted by CONSULTANT and shall render decisions pertaining 
thereto promptly, to avoid unreasonable delay in the progress of CONSULTANT's work.

SECTION 4 - TERM 
The services to be performed under this Agreement shall commence on issuance of the Notice to 
Proceed (“NTP”) and be completed within 12 months of issuance of the NTP. 
SECTION 5 - PAYMENT 
Payment shall be made by CITY only for services rendered and upon submission of a payment 
request upon completion and CITY approval of the work performed.  In consideration for the full 
performance of the services set forth in Exhibit A, CITY agrees to pay CONSULTANT a fee in an amount of 
$150,000, pursuant to rates stated in Exhibit A to this Agreement, attached and incorporated by 
reference. 
SECTION 6 - TERMINATION 
Without limitation to such rights or remedies as CITY shall otherwise have by law, CITY shall have 
the right to terminate this Agreement or suspend work on the Project for any reason, upon ten (10) days' 
written notice to CONSULTANT.  CONSULTANT agrees to cease all work under this Agreement upon receipt 
of said written notice. 
Upon termination and upon CITY's payment of the amount required to be paid, documents 
become the property of CITY, and CONSULTANT shall transfer them to CITY upon request without 
additional compensation. 
SECTION 7 - OWNERSHIP OF DOCUMENTS 
All documents prepared by CONSULTANT in the performance of this Agreement, although 
instruments of professional service, are and shall be the property of CITY, whether the project for which 
they are made is executed or not. 
SECTION 8 - CONFIDENTIALITY 
All reports and documents prepared by CONSULTANT in connection with the performance of this 
Agreement are confidential until released by CITY to the public.  CONSULTANT shall not make any such 
documents or information available to any individual or organization not employed by CONSULTANT or 
CITY without the written consent of CITY before any such release. 
SECTION 9 - INTEREST OF CONSULTANT 
CONSULTANT covenants that it presently has no interest, and shall not acquire any interest, 
direct or indirect, financial or otherwise, which would conflict in any manner or degree with the 
performance of the services under this Agreement.

result is accomplished.  Nothing herein shall be construed to make CONSULTANT an agent or employee 
of CITY while providing services under this Agreement. 
SECTION 11 - INDEMNITY 
CONSULTANT agrees to hold harmless and indemnify CITY, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents from and against any and all claims, loss, liability, damage, and expense arising

negligence or willful misconduct.  CONSULTANT agrees to defend City, its elected and appointed officials, 
employees, and agents against any such claims. 
SECTION 12 - INSURANCE 
CONSULTANT shall procure and maintain for the duration of the contract and three years 
thereafter (five years for building or major improvements) the insurance specified in Exhibit B to this 
Agreement. 
SECTION 13 - NON-ASSIGNABILITY

and cannot be transferred, assigned, or subcontracted by CONSULTANT without the prior written 
consent of CITY.

SKILL OF CONSULTANT

skilled in the professional calling necessary to perform the work agreed to be done under this Agreement 
and that CITY relies upon the skill of CONSULTANT to do and perform the work in the most skillful 
manner, and CONSULTANT agrees to thus perform the work.  The acceptance of CONSULTANT's work by 
CITY does not operate as a release of CONSULTANT from said obligation. 
SECTION 15 - WAIVERS 
The waiver by either party of any breach or violation of any term, covenant, or condition of this 
Agreement or of any provisions of any ordinance or law shall not be deemed to be a waiver of such term, 
covenant, condition, ordinance or law or of any subsequent breach or violation of the same or of any 
other term, condition, ordinance, or law.  The subsequent acceptance by either party of any fee or other 
money which may become due hereunder shall not be deemed to be a waiver of any preceding breach 
or violation by the other party of any term, covenant, or condition of this Agreement or of any applicable 
law or ordinance.

--- page 179 ---
SECTION 16 - SEVERABILITY 
If any term or portion of this Agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or otherwise unenforceable 
by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall continue in full 
force and effect. 
SECTION 17 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES 
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing of 
a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement.  
The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, appellate court, 
or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It is the intent of this 
Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for attorney fees arising out 
of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement. 
SECTION 18 - NON-DISCRIMINATION 
CONSULTANT warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with applicable 
regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONSULTANT nor any of its 
subcontractors shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act. 
SECTION 19 - MEDIATION 
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw. 
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation and 
making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once commenced 
by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but not more than 60 
days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties. 
SECTION 20 - LITIGATION 
CONSULTANT shall testify at CITY'S request if litigation is brought against CITY in connection with 
CONSULTANT's services under this Agreement.  Unless the action is brought by CONSULTANT, or is based 
upon CONSULTANT's wrongdoing, CITY shall compensate CONSULTANT for preparation for testimony, 
testimony, and travel at CONSULTANT's standard hourly rates at the time of actual testimony. 
SECTION 21 - NOTICES 
All notices hereunder shall be given in writing and mailed, postage prepaid, addressed as follows:

Proposal, City of San Mateo, Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 3 Project

PROJECT UNDERSTANDING 
The City is seeking an experienced consultant to provide construction inspection and materials testing services for the 
Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 3 Project. The streets that are included in this package include Sunnybrae 
Boulevard, Folkstone Avenue, Guildford Avenue, Hawthorne Drive, Birch Avenue, E. Santa Inez Avenue, N. Idaho Street, 
State Street and Norton Street. The work includes temporary traffic and pedestrian control, wedge grind and HMA 
overlay, mill and install new HMA pavement, base failure repairs, PCC failure repairs, curb ramps, removal and 
replacement of curb & gutter and sidewalk, sign installation, striping, and installation of street lighting conduit, pullboxes 
and conductors. Ghirardelli understands the project was designed by Wilsey Ham with an overall engineer’s estimate of 
approximately $4,702,000. The project is anticipated to start in August 2022 with a total of 100 construction working 
days. 
 
SCOPE OF WORK 
Ghirardelli is proud to bring its experience with similar street reconstruction projects. Our approach for this type of 
project is built around the qualifications and experience of our staff and lessons learned from previous projects with the 
City of San Mateo. Our team realizes that the success of any construction project depends on the ability of the 
construction management team to proactively identify and anticipate issues and resolve these potential issues promptly 
before they impact the project budget and schedule. 
 
Ghirardelli will provide construction inspection services on the project, including, but not limited to the following: 
 
• 
Inspect and observe construction contractor’s work daily for compliance with the contract documents, 
environmental documents, state laws, regulations, etc. 
• 
Provide construction contractor with acceptability or deficiencies in work and required actions for contract 
compliance. 
• 
Maintain daily inspection reports of work, including photos, descriptions of daily activities, any corrective 
actions to be taken by the Contractor, persons and equipment onsite, and any other pertinent information. 
• 
Review of materials and equipment delivered to site for compliance with submittals and contract 
documents. 
• 
Coordinate material testing by others as required. 
• 
Review of construction contractor’s compliance with all permits, stormwater regulations and permits 
(SWPPP), and environmental documents. 
• 
Review of construction contractor’s compliance with health and safety standards and regulations and 
reporting of non-compliance. 
• 
Monitor record documents to ensure proper maintenance and completion by contractor. 
• 
Develop punch list and perform other project closeout activities, as needed. 
 
Daily Inspection Reports 
Ghirardelli’s inspector (Kean Tran) will prepare a daily report for each construction day. The inspector’s daily reports will 
be provided to the City’s Resident Engineer. This information will be used for the preparation and evaluation of the 
monthly progress estimates, non-compliance issues, construction delays, compliance tests, CPM schedule review, 
liquidated damages, and claims. The daily reports will include the following information: 
 
• 
Date, project identification, contractor’s name, subcontractor’s name(s), and time of inspections. 
• 
Conditions (weather, moisture, soil, etc.); any inclement weather conditions that hampered or delayed the 
contractor’s operations. 
• 
Activities detail. Scheduled activities including starting and completion dates. Bid item numbers of all 
activities should be listed in the daily report. 
• 
Difficulties encountered by the contractor. 
• 
Controversial matters (disputes, questionable items, etc.). 
183 of 615

--- page 184 ---
Proposal, City of San Mateo, Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 3 Project 
www.ghirardelliassoc.com 
 
 
• 
Deficiencies and violations (including construction work, safety, labor, etc.). 
• 
Major material and equipment deliveries to the site (type, quantity, means of delivery, and condition of 
materials). 
• 
Record of manpower, construction equipment, and materials testing locations and results.

our fee will be $148,827.40.

dynamic compaction, wick drain installations, joint and other utilities and storm drain installations across property, easement 
and jurisdictional boundaries; road construction, storm drain outfall; temporary and permanent traffic signage and signals and 
traffic control in a highly industrialized operating seaport area. As construction inspector, Mike monitored and reported on the 
contractor’s field activities. He managed and ordered QA special inspection and material testing lab staff as required to confirm 
construction compliance with the plans and specifications. Enforcement extended to environmental and safety elements of 
the construction work. 
 
 
Oakland Airport Connector 
BART; Oakland, CA 
Construction Inspector | The Oakland Airport Connector provided a high-quality link between BART's regional rail

MICHAEL WILLIAMS 
CONSTRUCTION INSPECTOR 
system, and the Oakland Airport, using a direct and exclusive aerial guide-way for transit vehicles departing to, and from 
the existing Coliseum station. Mr. Williams provided quality assurance of contractor’s work, prepared daily diaries, 
assisted with monthly pay estimates, contract administration, photo documentation, tracked and verified all work, paid 
close attention to public and worker safety. He also tracked hazardous materials and recyclable materials, recorded work 
progress, and documented contractor activities to verify the project was completed in accordance with the contract 
documents, and regulatory agency requirements. Mr. Williams also monitored the work to help ensure that dust control, 
SWPPP, haul-routes and all other plans were followed. 
 
 
Central Subway Project 
San Francisco Municipal Transportation Agency; San Francisco, CA 
Quality Control Inspector | The Central Subway Project extended the Muni Metro T Third Line to provide a direct transit 
link between the Bayshore and Mission Bay areas to SoMa, downtown, and Chinatown. Once the Central Subway is 
completed, T Third Line trains will travel mostly underground from the 4th Street Caltrain Station to Chinatown, bypassing 
heavy traffic on congested 4th Street and Stockton Street. Mr. Williams completed daily logs and inspection reports along 
with calculations for quantities of materials, monitored construction activities for contract compliance and performed 
controlled inspections, and reviewed and interpreted drawings, contract specifications, material lists and samples of 
materials for compliance with contract requirements and codes. He also worked with project managers and Inspectors of 
Records to verify work and materials meet code and plan specifications. 
 
 
Transbay Tube CP Anode Sled Replacement 
BART; Oakland, CA 
Construction Inspector | The work included demolition of 15 existing rectifier alarm assemblies and one existing junction 
box. The scope included removal of old and installation of 12 new permanent reference cells, four new electrical 
resistance corrosometer probes, and installation of 16 new cathodic protection monitoring panels. Work also included 
providing associated conduits, cabling, electrical connections, and monitoring equipment required for a complete system. 
This project was performed in the Transbay Tube Upper and Lower Gallery and was a Security Sensitive Information 
Project. Mr. Williams worked on the barge in the Bay and in the Transbay Tube. He was responsible for verifying conditions 
of the anode cable; inspecting splicing and conducting continuity tests for the anode cable; verifying the coordinates of the 
cable replacement; documenting project photos; providing written documentation of work being performed; verifying 
replacement of drain valves; and coordinating between the contractor, divers and the Resident Engineer on the project. 
 
 
Traction Power Substation Replacement - ASL 
BART; San Leandro, CA

five locations. The major TPSS equipment was procured and delivered under a separate Procurement Contract No. 15EK-
210. Mr. Williams monitored the contractor’s work for compliance with the contract documents and verified that the 
work was performed per the approved SSWP. He also prepared daily reports to document the contractor’s activities.

EXHIBIT B 
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS 
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE  
Coverage shall be at least as broad as: 
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal &
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate
limit applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or
the general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit.
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if
CONSULTANT has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no
less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage.
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily
injury or disease.
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and 
shall be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT. 
Other Insurance Provisions 
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions: 
Additional Insured Status 
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used).

Primary Coverage 
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it. 
Notice of Cancellation 
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City. 
Waiver of Subrogation 
CONSULTANT hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said 
CONSULTANT may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  
CONSULTANT agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of 
subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of 
subrogation endorsement from the insurer.   
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions 
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the CONSULTANT to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof 
of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within 
the retention.   
Acceptability of Insurers 
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City. 
Verification of Coverage 
CONSULTANT shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies 
of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the CONSULTANT’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

SUBJECT:
Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 3 Project – Amendment
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve Amendment No. 4 to the agreement with Wilsey Ham for engineering services during construction in an amount 
not to exceed $134,000 for a new agreement total of $528,874; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute the 
amendment in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
On September 20, 2021, City Council approved an agreement with Wilsey Ham in the amount of $354,000 to provide 
professional design services for the Citywide Street Reconstruction Phase 3 Project (Project). Amendment Nos. 1, 2, and 3 
to the agreement were issued for additional design services on the Project utilizing available contingency and consistent 
with the authority granted by Council.  Amendment Nos. 1, 2, and 3 utilized $40,874 of the contingency amount, with $126 
of the contingency remaining. 
As discussed in the September 20, 2021 agenda report, staff anticipated Wilsey Ham would also provide engineering 
services during construction of the Project, and the scope and fee would be negotiated and brought back to City Council 
for approval.  Staff received a proposal from Wilsey Ham for engineering services during construction for the Project in the 
amount of $134,000 and finds the scope, budget, and schedule are reasonable and in alignment with the City’s needs 
during construction. Accordingly, staff recommends approving Amendment No. 4 to the agreement with Wilsey Ham.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Sufficient funds for this amendment are available in the Citywide Street Reconstruction Package 1/Phase 3 Project 
(#467771). 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This project is categorically exempt from CEQA review as an “existing facility,” because it consists of the operation, repair, 
maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures, facilities, 
mechanical equipment, or topographical features, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. (CEQA 
Guidelines Section 15301.)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

EXHIBIT A-4 
SCOPE OF SERVICES 
PROJECT DESCRIPTION 
CONSULTANT is providing engineering design services for the Citywide Street Reconstruction Project, 
Phase 6 which is part of the City’s Smooth Streets Program (SSP). The City desires to retain the 
CONSULTANT to provide Engineering Services During Construction (ESDC) which includes both bid 
and construction services.  
 
SCOPE OF WORK 
 
Task 1 –Construction Services 
 
The following work is included in this task: 
 
• 
Attend one (1) pre-construction meeting. 
• 
Perform proof rolling evaluation of pavement (up to 3 days assumed) 
• 
Visit site during construction (up to 9 visits assumed). 
• 
Review and respond to submittals (up to 10 submittals assumed). 
• 
Review and respond to Requests for Information (up to 18 RFI’s assumed). 
• 
Construction Meetings (assumed 20 weekly meetings). 
• 
Coordination with the Contractor and construction manager. 
• 
Assist the City with Contractor change order requests by reviewing estimates and providing 
input as to the technical merit of change order requests. 
• 
Design minor revisions as necessary to accommodate field conditions. 
• 
Perform final walk-through inspection and prepare punch list. Prepare record drawings. 
• 
Geotechnical field observation services (up to 18, eight-hour field visits) and preparation of Daily 
Field Reports. 
• 
Street light conduit submittal review (up to 4 assumed), RFI response (up to 2 assumed),and 
assistance to the City regarding Contractor change order requests. 
 
Deliverables: Responses to bidders’ questions and addendums in electronic and hard copy formats. 
Responses to RFI’s in PDF format. Sketches and plan revisions, as required by field conditions. 
Contractor change orders, as necessary. Daily Field Reports provided by Fugro (geotechnical sub-
consultant). Punch list memo. 
 
Task 2 – Additional Pavement Design Services (If Authorized by CITY) 
 
If the areas showing pavement deflection are observed to be significantly larger than anticipated and 
authorized in writing by the CITY, CONSULTANT will re-design the proposed pavement construction 
methods, as necessary. The following work is included in this task: 
 
• 
Attend design coordination meetings.  
• 
Update Improvement Plan to show base repair areas.  
• 
Update Details with new pavement reconstruction sections.  
• 
Review submittals for alternative pavement materials (up to 5 submittals assumed).  
• 
Respond to alternative pavement RFIs (up to 5 RFIs assumed).  
• 
Geotechnical re-designs of pavement sections and construction methods. Attendance of design 
strategy meetings with City and design team. Design consultation. 
 
Deliverables: Updated plan, submittal reviews, and RFI responses will be provided in digital format 
(PDF). 
 
196 of 615

--- page 197 ---
Assumptions and Exclusions 
The following assumptions and exclusions were used in the preparation of this proposal: 
1. All permit costs and agency/utility fees will be paid by others. 
2. CONSULTANT will submit earned value analysis (summary of hours and work performed by 
classification) when submitting monthly invoicing. 
3. Any design coordination meetings will be attended virtually (video or phone). 
4. Items not included in the scope above are excluded. 
 
SCHEDULE 
CONSULTANT will provide the scope of services described above on a mutually agreeable schedule. 
 
BUDGET 
CONSULTANT proposes to provide services on a time and materials basis with a not-to-exceed budget 
of $134,000 in accordance with the 2022 City of San Mateo Fee Schedule, effective through December 
31, 2022.

SUBJECT:
Right-of-Way Permitting Support Services – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with Anchor Engineering to provide right-of-way permitting review and processing services in the 
amount of $500,000; and authorize the Director of Public Works to execute the agreement in substantially the form 
presented.
BACKGROUND:
Encroachment permit scopes range from simple sidewalk repairs to complex utility installations and relocations, requiring 
significant road closures and detours impacting the public right-of-way. The Public Works Right-of-Way Division (Public 
Works) has seen a steady increase in encroachment permits in the last five (5) years. In 2017, Public Works processed a 
total of 790 encroachment permits, and for 2022, the Public Works estimates processing over 1,200 encroachment 
permits. This results in an estimated increase of 52% in the number of encroachment permits that must be reviewed by 
the three (3) Engineering Technicians assigned to process the permits. 
Wireless Communication Facilities Permits
In 2021, Public Works updated San Mateo Municipal Code (SMMC) Chapter 17.10 Wireless Communication Facilities in the 
Public Right-of-Way and the Design Standards in preparation for an increase in Wireless Communications Facilities (WCF) 
permit applications. WCF permits are a type of encroachment permit that require significant staff resources to review 
when compared to other types of encroachment permits. The City currently has four master license agreements with 
telecommunications companies. The City recently met with one of those telecommunication companies that is ready to 
submit approximately 200 WCF applications, which are in addition to the number of regular encroachment permit 
applications Public Works expects to receive. Public Works estimates hundreds of future WCF applications will be 
submitted from all telecommunication companies. 
As required by the Federal Communications Commission (FCC), City staff must review WCF applications within 90 days (60 
days for installations on existing structures); the review time frame is also known as the “shot clock.” The shot clock starts 
when the applicant submits the application and stops when the permit is approved. If staff cannot complete the review 
within the shot clock, the application is automatically deemed approved by existing federal law. To pay for all the costs 
associated with City staff time, consultant review, and processing of WCF applications, the fiscal year 2022-23 fee schedule 
charges a minimum deposit of $2,500 or $4,000 depending on the type of WCF application. 
Support Services for Wireless Communication Facilities Permits Review and Processing
With the existing staff and encroachment permit workload, Public Works does not have sufficient resources to review and 
process all the anticipated WCF applications within the timeframe allowed by the FCC shot clock. To augment City 
198 of 615

--- page 199 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
resources, Public Works solicited proposals from two firms, CSG Consultants and Anchor Engineering, for professional 
services to review and process WCF applications. The City received one proposal from Anchor Engineering. After reviewing 
Anchor Engineering’s proposal, Public Works confirmed that they are qualified to review and process WCF applications. 
 Based on the foregoing, staff recommends approval of an agreement with Anchor Engineering to provide permitting 
review and processing services.
BUDGET IMPACT:
Funding for Anchor Engineering will be full cost recovery as they will be charging to the deposit set forth in the most 
current adopted fee schedule. 
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This agreement is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is a government fiscal activity which does not involve any 
commitment to any specific project which may result in a potentially significant physical impact on the environment. 
(CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

If any term or portion of this Agreement is held to be invalid, illegal, or otherwise 
unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall 
continue in full force and effect. 
 
SECTION 17 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES 
 
 
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement. 
 
SECTION 18 - NON-DISCRIMINATION 
 
 
CONSULTANT warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONSULTANT nor any of its 
subcontractors shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act. 
 
SECTION 19 - MEDIATION 
 
 
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw. 
 
 
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once

CONSULTANT and supersedes all prior negotiations, representations, and agreements, either written 
or oral. 
 
 
This document may be amended only by written instrument, signed by both CITY and 
CONSULTANT. 
 
SECTION 23 - AUTHORITY TO ENTER INTO AGREEMENT 
 
 
CONSULTANT has all requisite power and authority to conduct its business and to execute, 
deliver, and perform the Agreement.  Each party warrants that the individuals who have signed this 
Agreement have the legal power, right, and authority to make this Agreement and to bind each 
respective party.
