ATTACHMENT A SCOPE OF SERVICES 
 
ATTACHMENT A 
Scope of Services 
 
The Consultant will provide consulting services for sanitary sewer rehabilitation for all project elements 
listed in this Scope of Services. The project and fee negotiations will be executed in three phases for the 
sewer rehabilitation project as shown below and further defined in this Scope of Services: 
 
 
Phase 1 
Task 1 ‐ General Services 
Task 2 ‐ Video Inspection & Cleaning Services 
Task 3 – Evaluation of Video Inspection Data 
 
Phase 2 
Task 1 ‐ General Services 
Task 4 – Project Design and Construction Documents 
Task 5 – Bid Support 
 
Phase 3 
Task 1 – General Services 
Task 6 – Construction Phase Services 
 
See the chart below for the approximate number of manholes and length of pipe located within Basins C, 
D, El Camino Real, and Basin A: 
Basin 
Manholes 
Pipes (LF) 
C 
934 
172,768 
D 
1,168 
246,294 
ECR 
153 
57,801 
A 
1,118 
271,769 
Typical tasks that are required of the Consultant under these categories are detailed in the following 
sections. Throughout the design of this project, the Consultant will submit deliverables for review and 
approval by the City and other governing agencies as required. A preliminary description of proposed 
deliverables has been provided in this Scope of Services and will be finalized during contract and fee 
negotiations. 
The deliverables required by the Consultant will vary depending on the necessary improvements to the 
sewer lines defined for each basin. All drawings and maps shall be developed using AutoCAD, ArcGIS 
and PDFs capable of plotting at a legible scale, on both 11x17” and 22x34” sheets. 
Additional deliverable information is provided in the various task descriptions below. 
For the 60%, 90%, and 100% design submittals, the Consultant shall attend a review meeting with City 
staff where the submittals will be presented to the City and will demonstrate that previous City 
comments have been addressed in the submittal. 
 
The City reserves the right to discontinue, alter, or postpone services at any time. 
 
Task 1 – General Services 
Project management and coordination include tasks that will be performed by the Consultant 
302 of 615

--- page 303 ---
ATTACHMENT A SCOPE OF SERVICES 
 
throughout the project. Task 1 will cover all general services required for Phase 1, Phase 2, and Phase 3 
for the sewer line rehabilitation project. These services include: 
 
General Project Management and Coordination 
 
Risk Management 
 
Workshops and Meetings 
 
Management of Subconsultants and Subcontractors 
 
Agency Coordination and City Meeting Support 
Further information regarding expectations for these services will be issued as a separate program 
standard in the Design Standards and Guidelines, prior to the first phase negotiations. 
 
Task 1.1 – Project Management 
Task 1.1.1 – General Management 
The Consultant will be responsible for detailed management of the project, including subconsultants 
and subcontractors, and will keep the City appraised of the status of the project. 
The Consultant will provide the key management personnel as described in their proposal on this 
project. The Consultant will not reassign the key project personnel without prior approval of the City per 
the Standard Agreement for Professional Consultant Services. 
Task 1.1.2 – Progress Report 
The Consultant will prepare and submit monthly progress reports that include a narrative of the work 
completed by task, project action item log, upcoming work, project issues, budget and schedule status, 
percent complete, cost to complete, earned value versus actual spent analysis, potential scope or 
budget changes, and other important project information. The monthly progress report will be 
submitted with the project invoice as part of the monthly request for payment. The invoices will 
comply with Program standards. The invoices will document the man‐hours and billing rates for staff 
for each task in the WBS, as well as overhead, profit and any direct costs. The current and previous 
billing period invoicing, as well as a summary of the budget spent, budget remaining, cost to complete, 
and all backup documentation for expenses will be included. The monthly progress report and project 
schedule will be submitted with the project invoice as part of the monthly request for payment. 
 
Task 1.2 – Risk Management 
The Consultant will participate in the development and maintenance of a set of project risks in 
coordination with the Project Manager and the Program’s risk management team. The Consultant shall 
review the risks and mitigation measures previously identified by the Program and determine if 
additional risks or mitigations are required. Consultant shall implement any risk mitigations assigned to 
them as part of the risk development process. If both the City and the Consultant determine that a 
meeting is required, a risk review meeting will be conducted within one month after Notice to Proceed 
and will be addressed as needed throughout the course of the project. This meeting will be about one 
hour in length and may be combined with other meetings between the City and the Consultant.  
Task 1.3 – Workshops and Meetings 
The Consultant will prepare and submit to the City an agenda and meeting minutes for each formal 
meeting or workshop and hold progress meetings. Assume monthly meetings for CCTV work, monthly for 
design work and monthly for services during construction. 
Task 1.4 – Quality Control 
The Consultant will develop a Quality Assurance/Quality Control (QA/QC) Plan for implementation of the 
Scope of Work. The Consultant’s QA/QC Plan will be reviewed and approved by the City Project Manager 
303 of 615

--- page 304 ---
ATTACHMENT A SCOPE OF SERVICES 
 
and will include or reference all the controls necessary for implementation. QA/QC Plans shall include 
detailed requirements for providing the required integrity for all field work, including conducting the 
explorations, sampling, and sample custody and transport. All QA/QC procedures for laboratory work 
shall be included. Each submittal, or partial submittal of testing results shall be accompanied by a written 
certification, signed by the Consultant’s Project Manager and the responsible individual from the 
applicable subconsultant, stating that all QA/QC procedures have been complied with for the information 
or submittal being delivered to the City. 
Submittals that do not include the written certification of compliance or that contain gross deficiencies 
or errors requiring a significant amount of the City staff time for checking will be returned without 
review until the City is satisfied that a thorough Consultant’s review, compliance with standards, 
checking and correction for coherence, consistency, spelling,  etc., has been performed. 
Task 1.5 – Management of Subconsultants 
If the Consultant requires the services of any subconsultants, then the Consultant will include a list of 
them in the proposal, explain why they are needed, and provide a general description of their effort. 
The Consultant will manage the activities of subconsultants during performance of the project. All 
requirements of the contract shall also apply to subconsultants. 
Task 1.6 – Agency Coordination 
The Consultant will assist the City in coordinating the project with various local/county, state/regional, 
and federal agencies. This task includes attending meetings, as needed, with applicable agencies, 
providing meeting minutes, and providing project information and exhibits to the agencies. This task is 
established as an allowance task and shall only be used when specifically requested by the City in 
writing. Costs for this task shall be invoiced as a separate line item and shall include a copy of the 
authorization from the City. 
Task 2 –Video Inspection and Cleaning Services 
This task should be conducted independently, on an as‐needed basis as determined by the City, for 
each sewer rehabilitation project awarded to the Consultant. 
Task 2.1 Plans 
For the sewer rehabilitation projects where the City has deemed video inspection and cleaning 
services are required, the Consultant will create Plans showing all sewer lines and manholes within 
project limits, as well as a Traffic Control Plan.  The City will provide the Consultant with project limits, 
current GIS data, and sample Plans.  The Consultant will provide drafts of the Plans until City 
reviewers are satisfied and the project has been deemed ready for the Consultant’s CCTV 
subcontractor to perform the work. The Consultant’s subcontractor should follow City Standard CCTV 
Guidelines. Refer to the City of San Mateo Sewer System Management Plan (SSMP), Appendix 4.2. 
Task 2.2 Pipe and Manhole Inspections and Cleaning 
The Consultant will manage their CCTV subconsultant while the investigation and cleaning operations 
occur. The Prime Consultant or CCTV subconsultant shall be NASSCO PACP and MACP certified in 
order to perform pipe and manhole inspections. Ideally the Prime Consultant is also NASSCO certified; 
however, it is sufficient if only the CCTV subconsultant performing the inspections is certified. 
Consultant shall provide inspection videos and reports to the City as they receive them from their 
CCTV subcontractor. Consultant will begin Task 3 operations concurrently with Task 2.2 for the same 
project.   
Task 3 – Evaluation of Video Inspection Data 
304 of 615

--- page 305 ---
ATTACHMENT A SCOPE OF SERVICES 
 
This task should be conducted independently for each sewer rehabilitation project awarded to the 
Consultant. 
Task 3.1 Evaluation Technical Memorandum 
The consultant will be responsible for reviewing and evaluating the video inspection data provided to 
them for sanitary sewer mains and manholes. The consultant will then prioritize repairs based on the 
available project budget, the urgency of the repairs, and the value of the repair to maintenance and 
operation of the sewer system with the City’s input. 
The City will share a list and/or map of roads that are under pavement excavation moratorium, as 
well as those roads which are scheduled for paving work. The consultant will identify if any of the 
proposed sewer repairs overlap with these road segments. 
The results of this evaluation shall be included in a technical memorandum provided to the City. 
 Task 3.2 GIS Deliverable 
The consultant shall provide the City with a GIS file containing all recommended repairs upon completion 
of the Evaluation Technical Memorandum. 
Task 4 – Project Design and Construction Documents 
This task should be conducted independently for each sewer rehabilitation project awarded to the 
Consultant.  Consultant shall design repairs or improvements resulting from the inspection 
evaluation. Consultant shall provide drawings for each repair or improvement and compile them into 
one or more project packages as agreed with the City. The design work entails taking the video 
inspection evaluation (Task 3) and designing the changes that will be required to repair any broken 
pipes or manholes. 
Task 4.1 – 60% Design Development 
The design activities and design elements shall become more concrete once the projects are assigned 
and shall be at a 60% level of completion. 
A statement signed by Consultant’s Project Manager that all deliverables have been reviewed or 
otherwise checked in full conformance with the Consultants QA/QC Plan will be required. 
 
All design work shall be in conformance with the Clean Water Program design guidelines and standards 
which will be provided to the Consultant during the negotiation phase. 
The 60% design deliverable shall include  one print ready PDF on electronic media acceptable to the 
City of all deliverables. 
Task 4.1.1 – Field Investigations 
The Consultant shall conduct field investigations as required to verify if there are any potential impacts 
to/from surrounding features that need to be addressed in the design for each sewer rehab location. 
The City will provide ArcGIS information in relation to the specific locations that is identified for rehab to 
the Consultant for use on the project. 
The field investigation shall note all obstructions that may be affected by the needed improvements. 
The Consultant shall conduct a thorough investigation at easement locations, which includes taking 
pictures of upstream and downstream manholes, and documenting any obstacles in the way. The 
Consultant will be responsible for obtaining all permits and access agreements to perform the survey, 
as well as providing any necessary traffic control. 
This work shall be completed before commencing with Task 4.2. 
305 of 615

--- page 306 ---
ATTACHMENT A SCOPE OF SERVICES 
 
If a Subconsultant is used for this work, the Consultant will identify the Subconsultant with the response 
to this RFP. 
Task 4.1.2 – 60% Design Submittal 
The design submittal at 60% shall include initial design drawings, calculations, databases, specifications, 
schedule and any other project documentation to support this submittal. Consultant shall provide plan 
and profile drawings for any segment where the repairs cross roadways, or additional locations as 
requested by the City. Point repairs (i.e. 6’ length repairs) in roadways will not be required to be 
designed with plan and profile drawings, unless specifically requested by the City. 
Task 4.2 – 90% Design Development 
This task shall be conducted independently for each sewer rehabilitation project awarded to the 
Consultant. All design work shall be in conformance with the Clean Water Program design guidelines and 
standards, which will be provided to the Consultant during the negotiation phase. 
For the 90% Design Submittal, the major design elements shall be well‐established and essentially 
complete. All supplementary/auxiliary design elements shall be incorporated between the 60% and 90% 
Design Submittals for a complete design package. Drawings for all disciplines shall be substantially 
complete. Specifications shall be substantially complete and include all sections and all divisions 
including the legal, procedural, and general “front end” sections. 
This submittal will include all finished, checked and completed plans and specifications. No new 
drawings and/or specification sections by the Consultant will be expected after this stage. All City 
comments on the previous submittal will have been resolved, rejected, addressed and/or incorporated 
in this submittal. The project is essentially finished and submitted as complete. The only missing effort is 
the City final review and regulatory approval with comment incorporation. 
Consultant’s checking will include a review of all deliverables within each discipline (i.e. drawings, detailed 
specifications, project support documentation, etc.) and an inter‐discipline coordination cross‐check to 
correct discrepancies among the project documents. 
Discipline calculations, databases, construction cost estimates, schedule, and other project support 
documentation will be updated and submitted to the City to reflect the status of the Drawings and 
Detailed Specifications and will support the 90% design submittal workshops. 
A construction cost estimate (AACE Class 2) with basis of estimate narrative and construction 
sequencing plan and schedule shall be included with the deliverables. 
The 90% design deliverable shall include a statement signed by Consultant’s Project Manager that all 
deliverables have been reviewed or otherwise checked and that they are in full conformance with 
Consultant’s QA/QC Plan. Additionally, the City will require the Consultant to fill out and sign the City’s 
QC Certification form. 
 
The 90% design deliverable shall include one print‐ready PDF on electronic media acceptable to the 
City of all deliverables. 
Task 4.3 – 100% Design and Bid Documents 
This task shall be conducted independently for each identified project(s) awarded to the Consultant. All 
design work shall be in conformance with the Clean Water Program design guidelines and standards, 
which will be provided to the Consultant during the negotiation phase. 
Following receipt of all City and regulatory review comments on the 90% Design Submittals, the 
Consultant will prepare a Final (100%) Design and will submit the 100% design submittal as a final check 
set. The final check set shall include all bid documents. Prior to submittal, the Consultant will have 
performed an inter‐disciplinary cross‐check of the design changes made since the 90% design submittal 
306 of 615

--- page 307 ---
ATTACHMENT A SCOPE OF SERVICES 
 
and corrected the related discrepancies. The Consultant will not add any new drawings or make any 
changes to the plans or specifications that are not a result of comments received by the City, regulatory 
review, or a final in‐house quality control check. Consultant and City shall conduct a workshop to verify 
that all City comments have been addressed and all permit conditions are accounted for. After the City’s 
back‐check review is complete, Consultant shall revise the documents and submit Final Bid Documents. 
The 100% back‐check design deliverable shall include a statement signed by Consultant’s Project Manager 
that all deliverables have been reviewed or otherwise checked in full conformance with Consultant’s 
QA/QC Plan. 
The 100% back‐check design deliverable shall include 4 hard copies and one print‐ready PDF on electronic 
media acceptable to the City of all deliverables. 
The Final Bid Documents will include finished, checked and completed plans and specifications, and will 
incorporate all City comments from previous submittals. The Consultant will document in‐house changes 
and submit them to the City along with the Final Bid Documents. 
The Final Design submittals will also include all final databases, reports, construction schedules, cost 
estimates, engineering calculations and bid ready project plans and specifications. 
All plans, detailed specifications and engineering calculations will be stamped and signed by a Registered 
Engineer in the State of California. 
An updated construction cost estimate (AACE Class 1) and construction sequencing plan and schedule 
will be included with the deliverables. 
The Final Bid Documents deliverable shall include one print‐ready PDF on electronic media acceptable 
to the City of all deliverables.  
Task 5 – Bid Period Assistance 
This task shall be conducted independently for each identified project(s) awarded to the 
Consultant.  The Consultant shall provide assistance during the bid period, including the following: 
 
Participate in the pre‐bid conference, if necessary. 
 
Prepare responses to bidder’s questions. 
 
Prepare addenda to Bid Documents, as needed. 
 
Participate in reviewing alternate equipment and material proposals from the contractor, if 
applicable. 
 
Participate in the evaluation of the submitted bids, furnish consultation and advise to City staff, 
and assist with all the related equipment, cost and other analyses as required to finalize the 
award decision. 
 
Prepare conformed drawings and specifications that incorporate all addenda. The Final Conformed 
Documents deliverable shall include one print ready PDF on electronic media acceptable to the City. 
Task 6 – Construction Phase Services 
This task shall be conducted independently for each identified project(s) awarded to the Consultant. 
The Consultant shall provide services during construction, which may include the following: 
 
Attendance at pre‐construction conference and construction progress meetings 
 
General technical support 
 
Periodic field visits 
 
Submittal review 
307 of 615

--- page 308 ---
ATTACHMENT A SCOPE OF SERVICES 
 
 
Request for information responses and review 
 
Change order and contract document modifications preparation and review 
 
Progress payment application review 
 
Construction closeout support 
 
Preparation of record drawings

EXHIBIT C 
 
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS 
 
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE  
Coverage shall be at least as broad as: 
 
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products‐completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate 
limit applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or 
the general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit. 
 
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if 
CONSULTANT has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non‐owned autos (Code 9), with limit no 
less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage. 
 
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily 
injury or disease.  
 
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s 
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate 
 
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and 
shall be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT. 
 
Other Insurance Provisions 
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions: 
 
Additional Insured Status 
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used). 
 
Primary Coverage 
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self‐insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.

CONSULTANT hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said 
CONSULTANT may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  
CONSULTANT agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of 
subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of 
subrogation endorsement from the insurer.   
 
Deductibles and Self‐Insured Retentions 
Any deductibles or self‐insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the CONSULTANT to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof 
of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within 
the retention.   
 
Acceptability of Insurers 
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City. 
 
Verification of Coverage 
CONSULTANT shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies 
of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the CONSULTANT’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

CITY OF SAN MATEO

Agenda Number:  17
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR
Account Number: 10-4661
File ID: 22-6214
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
July 18, 2022 
SUBJECT:
Local Roadway Safety Plan – Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve an agreement with Kittelson & Associates, Inc. for preparation of the San Mateo Local Roadway Safety Plan in the 
amount of $120,938; approve a contingency in the amount of $12,000; and authorize the Public Works Director to execute 
the agreement and amendments within the contingency in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
The California Transportation Commission allocated $18 million of State funds in 2019 and 2020 for the purpose of 
assisting local agencies in developing Local Roadway Safety Plans (LRSPs) based on a new statewide requirement for cities 
to have an LRSP or equivalent plan to be eligible to apply for federal Highway Safety Improvement Program (HSIP) funds. 
LRSPs identify roadway safety emphasis areas, determine safety goals for reducing fatal and severe injury crashes, and 
identify, analyze, and prioritize safety improvements with the help of stakeholders representing the engineering, law 
enforcement, education, and emergency response personnel. The City was awarded a grant to develop an LRSP in the 
amount of $72,000 in grant funds that requires $8,000 in City matching funds for a total of $80,000. 
Staff first brought the concept of utilizing collision data for project prioritization to City Council during fall 2020. After 
several presentations on the subject, City Council concurred with staff’s recommendation of preparing an LRSP as the 
formalized method to identify traffic safety issues and recommend roadway improvements. Staff anticipates that the 
improvements recommended in the LRSP will be implemented throughout the City in conjunction with previously 
completed Traffic Action Plans. As such, and due to its citywide scale, staff desires to include a robust public outreach 
component during the LRSP preparation. The additional budget needed for public outreach support is estimated at 
approximately $41,000, which will be funded from the Neighborhood Traffic Management project, which was created to 
fund Traffic Action Plan implementation. 
On January 28, 2022, the Public Works Department issued a request for proposals (RFP) to select a qualified firm(s) to 
develop the LRSP. The City received proposals from five firms and evaluated the proposals based on each firm's 
understanding of the work involved, experience with similar work, quality of project team, and technical ability of key 
staff. Of the five firms, staff interviewed two firms for the project: Fehr & Peers and Kittelson & Associates, Inc. Following 
interviews with each proposed project team, staff found Kittelson & Associates, Inc. to be most qualified to complete the 
scope of work identified in the RFP. Staff recommends entering into an agreement with Kittelson & Associates, Inc. 
BUDGET IMPACT:
There are sufficient funds for this agreement between the Caltrans LRSP grant and Neighborhood Traffic Management 
project, which will fund the additional outreach and local match to complete the Local Roadway Safety Plan.

environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(4).)

SECTION 2 - DUTIES OF CONSULTANT

coordination of all work furnished by CONSULTANT under this Agreement.  CONSULTANT shall, without 
additional compensation, correct or revise any errors or deficiencies in its work. 
 
 
CONSULTANT represents that it is qualified to furnish the services described under this 
Agreement. 
 
 
CONSULTANT shall be responsible for employing or engaging all persons necessary to perform 
the services of CONSULTANT. 
 
CONSULTANT agrees to comply with the City’s minimum wage ordinance as codified in 
Municipal Code Chapter 5.92, which differs from the state minimum wage and includes a Consumer 
Price Index escalator.

unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, the remaining provisions of this Agreement shall

SECTION 17 - COSTS AND ATTORNEY FEES 
 
 
Attorney fees in total amount not exceeding $5000, shall be recoverable as costs (by the filing 
of a cost bill) by the prevailing party in any action or actions to enforce the provisions of this 
Agreement.  The above $5000 limit is the total of attorney fees recoverable whether in the trial court, 
appellate court, or otherwise, and regardless of the number of attorneys, trials, appeals, or actions.  It 
is the intent of this Agreement that neither party shall have to pay the other more than $5000 for 
attorney fees arising out of an action, or actions to enforce the provisions of this Agreement. 
 
SECTION 18 - NON-DISCRIMINATION 
 
 
CONSULTANT warrants that it is an Equal Opportunity Employer and shall comply with 
applicable regulations governing equal employment opportunity.  Neither CONSULTANT nor any of its 
subcontractors shall discriminate in the employment of any person because of race, color, national 
origin, ancestry, physical handicap, medical condition, marital status, sex, or age, unless based upon a 
bona fide occupational qualification pursuant to the California Fair Employment and Housing Act. 
 
SECTION 19 - MEDIATION 
 
 
Should any dispute arise out of this Agreement, any party may request that it be submitted to 
mediation.  The parties shall meet in mediation within 30 days of a request.  The mediator shall be 
agreed to by the mediating parties; in the absence of an agreement, the parties shall each submit one 
name from mediators listed by either the American Arbitration Association, the State Mediation and 
Conciliation Service, or other agreed-upon service.  The mediator shall be selected by a blind draw. 
 
 
The cost of mediation shall be borne equally by the parties.  Neither party shall be deemed the 
prevailing party.  No party shall be permitted to file a legal action without first meeting in mediation 
and making a good faith attempt to reach a mediated settlement.  The mediation process, once 
commenced by a meeting with the mediator, shall last until agreement is reached by the parties but 
not more than 60 days, unless the maximum time is extended by the parties.

Signing In Process
Scope of Services 
TASK 1. DETAILED WORK PLAN AND STRATEGY 
 
TASK 1 PURPOSE  
Facilitate regular communication with the City of San Mateo project manager and staff so that the 
LRSP development remains on schedule and meets the needs and interests of the City.  
Project Management 
As project manager, Anusha Musunuru will use the following approaches to ensure project success: 
Proactive Communication 
Routine communication between Anusha and San Mateo’s project manager will confirm that we 
are meeting or exceeding needs in our approach and performance of the work. 
Management of Team Members 
Anusha will define a detailed work plan for the consultant team at the time the contractual scope 
of work with City of San Mateo is established. The detailed work plan will identify internal team 
milestones related to quality control milestones as well as individuals responsible for conducting the 
work, reviewing the work, and confirming comments are adequately addressed. 
Managing Schedule 
Actively managing the schedule is critical. Anusha, Matt, and Damian will work with task leads to 
identify and provide the resources necessary to adhere to the established schedule.  
Controlling Allocation of Resources 
Anusha will help ensure that the appropriate allocation of resources is spent on each task by first 
establishing clear expectations with the San Mateo project manager and the task leads about 
desired outcomes. Furthermore, Kittelson’s internal budget tracking tools enable project managers 
to identify budget status of any project or task at any time during the week or day and will be 
applied to help control allocation of resources.  
Quality Deliverables 
We deeply care about the services we provide. Damian Stefanakis (Principal-in-charge) will assist 
with quality control and assurance, as will our team’s dedicated senior advisor, Matt Braughton.  
DocuSign Envelope ID: 09487D47-CA60-4581-A592-4AB64F1F522B
324 of 615

--- page 325 ---
Signing In Process
Task 1.1 Kick-off Meeting 
Kittelson will prepare for and conduct a project kick-off meeting with City of San Mateo project 
manager and staff to confirm schedule, scope, project objectives, goals, and key interests, and 
identify near-term data or other information needed. Kittelson will also request and obtain 
relevant City safety policies, programs, partners, and additional documentation from the City to 
document existing safety efforts and progress. This information will be included in the LRSP as 
required.  
Task 1.2 Monthly Progress Meetings, Progress Reports, and Invoices 
Kittelson’s project manager will conduct 30-minute monthly conference calls with the City. 
Based on the agenda and any topics for resolution, specific invites for local agency 
participation or other partners will be extended prior to the call. The focus of these calls will be to 
provide an update regarding ongoing work, identify any potential issues that require resolution, 
and provide a recurring opportunity for the City project manager to provide our team with 
feedback to be sure we are meeting or exceeding expectations.  
Based on the current conditions under the COVID-19 pandemic, we anticipate the kick-off 
meeting and all monthly progress meetings would be held virtually. If desired by the City and 
appropriate given the current local public health conditions, Kittelson may conduct the kickoff 
meeting in person at City offices. 
Task 1 Deliverables  
▪ 
Virtual kick-off meeting with City  
▪ 
Final work plan  
▪ 
Monthly virtual progress meetings, including meeting agendas and notes 
TASK 2. STAKEHOLDER AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT  
 
TASK 2 PURPOSE  
Establish a Project Development Team/Steering Committee comprised of multidisciplinary 
agency stakeholders and organizations and engage them throughout the process of 
developing the LRSP. Support City staff with preparing and presenting project-related materials 
for outreach and at public meetings. 
Task 2.1 Project Development Team Meetings 
Kittelson will work with City of San Mateo project manager and staff to identify potential safety 
partners to serve as part of the Project Development Team. We anticipate the roster for the PDT 
to include multidisciplinary partners which may include neighboring jurisdictions, Safe Routes to 
School program, disabled community, traffic safety advocacy groups, California Highway Patrol 
(CHP), Caltrans, San Mateo Police Department, and/or emergency services representatives. We 
also recommend including San Mateo school districts and representatives from San Mateo 
County Health to allow for a broader discussion of roadway safety. Ultimately, the invited 
participants of the PDT could become a regular steering committee to address roadway safety 
from a variety of perspectives for the City.  
Based on our prior experience with LRSPs and similar roadway safety plans, we propose two (2) 
Progress Meetings with the PDT at the following critical points in the LRSP development to 
provide the greatest benefit for the plan:  
DocuSign Envelope ID: 09487D47-CA60-4581-A592-4AB64F1F522B
325 of 615

--- page 326 ---
Signing In Process
▪ 
Progress Meeting #1: Introduction to the LRSP and safety management process, 
discussion of PDT member roles, discussion of crash analysis results, and priority locations.  
▪ 
Progress Meeting #2: Review the Draft LRSP and priority projects, identify changes for the 
Final City of San Mateo LRSP. 
Based on the current conditions under the COVID-19 pandemic, we anticipate that the initial 
meeting will be held virtually. If those conditions change and an in-person meeting is feasible, 
we will conduct these meetings in-person. Kittelson would coordinate the meeting dates and 
times. If it is feasible to hold either of these meetings as in-person meetings, we would work with 
City of San Mateo to identify an appropriate meeting space for the size of the group and 
accessible for all partners. Kittelson is well-versed at holding and facilitating stakeholder 
meetings virtually including coordinating and conducting breakout sessions as part of the 
meeting format. We bring a wide range of tools for virtual meetings including a variety of 
videoconference platforms (Zoom, Microsoft Team, etc.). We also regularly use visual tools 
including Concept Boards and Mural.Co that enable stakeholders to view and comment 
directly on content as others are also viewing and commenting. Those tools help simulate a 
workshop type of environment.  
For each meeting, Kittelson will prepare an agenda, meeting materials, facilitate the discussion 
and activities, take meeting notes, and disseminate the notes to attendees following the 
meeting. 
Task 2.2 Community Engagement Support 
Kittelson will work carefully with the City to tailor outreach activities to the unique characteristics 
of the city. We will integrate communication techniques, evaluation tools, technical expertise, 
and interactive mapping into the planning process to enhance safety analysis and evaluation. 
Throughout this project, we will structure our outreach approach around the belief that low-
income, minority status, or Limited English Proficiency (LEP) should never be a barrier to 
meaningful engagement.  
Task 2.2.1 Project Website  
Kittelson will develop and maintain a separate LRSP project website for the City of San Mateo. 
The City will have a unique URL (San Mateo name in the website address) that can be provided 
on the City’s website and social media platforms. The project website will have a landing page 
that will explain the LRSP process and reason the City is developing an LRSP. This website will 
house an interactive map that allows comments to be placed at specific locations, i.e., 
roadways or intersections throughout the City by the public. This website will remain live 
throughout the LRSP process and public comments will be summarized and addressed through 
the plan development.  
Task 2.2.2 Public Workshop  
Kittelson will prepare presentation exhibits and conduct one in-person public workshop, if 
desired by the City and appropriate given the current local public health conditions. This 
engagement phase is an opportunity to engage with public to present safety analysis results, 
gather public feedback on the LRSP and associated goals and strategies.  
 
 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 09487D47-CA60-4581-A592-4AB64F1F522B
326 of 615

--- page 327 ---
Signing In Process
Task 2.2.3 Selected Communities Public Engagement  
Not all roadway segments/intersections in the City have the same safety performance, crash 
history, or warrant similar treatments. Similarly, not all community groups within the City will 
require the same material, approach, or level of engagement. Kittelson has built this phase into 
the process because some places and community groups like downtown area and north 
central neighborhood will require an additional level of care and coordination.  
We will work with the City staff to identify “selected communities/groups” that are expected to 
be most challenging to gather input through the project website and public workshop. After 
identifying the selected communities, we will meet with public in these areas by organizing pop-
up events/presentation exhibits as part of any other event that’s happening in the 
neighborhood or in coordination with an existing community group meeting for use during 
targeted outreach. Kittelson will plan to host one (1) pop-up event/presentation exhibits and up 
to three (3) smaller scale pop-in/pop-up events in the City. These smaller scale pop-in/pop-up 
events could be at the farmers market, Hillsdale Mall, or at any non-profit organization events. 
This engagement phase is an opportunity to go and engage with public where they are to 
gather their feedback.    
Kittelson team will be prepared to schedule, coordinate, and lead all these meetings, however, 
we recognize that these meetings may be sensitive, and we will rely on the City staff to advise 
on which meetings should be scheduled and coordinated to pay special attention to these 
selected communities. Translation and interpretation services will be provided as necessary at 
the pop-up event/presentation exhibits.  
Task 2.2.4 Public Meetings 
Kittelson will assist City staff with preparing and presenting materials, answering questions, and 
providing notes for up to four (4) public meetings. It is anticipated that the first meeting would 
focus on presenting safety analysis results, second one on plan vision, goals, objectives, and 
emphasis areas, third one on priority locations, countermeasures, and non-engineering 
treatments, and the final one would be for LRSP approval at City Council. This scope assumes 
Kittelson staff virtual attendance at both public hearings. If desired by the City and appropriate 
given the current local public health conditions, Kittelson may attend the public meetings in 
person. 
Task 2 Deliverables  
▪ 
Attendance, facilitation, agendas, notes, and materials for up to two (2) PDT meetings  
▪ 
Project website and interactive map 
▪ 
Lead one (1) public workshop 
▪ 
Lead one (1) selected community engagement event 
▪ 
Lead up to three (3) smaller scale pop-in/pop-up events 
▪ 
Public comments summary and participation statistics 
▪ 
Attendance at up to four (4) public meetings  
▪ 
Presentation materials and meeting notes

TASK 3. BACKGROUND REVIEW, PLAN VISION, GOALS, AND 
OBJECTIVES  
 
TASK 3 PURPOSE  
Establish safety goals, priorities, and objectives for the roadway safety management process in 
the City. Develop measurable actions that align with the City’s goals and priorities. 
Kittelson will work with San Mateo project manager, city staff, and the PDT to create a vision, 
mission statement, and goals that guide the LRSP and align with City Council priorities, City’s 
existing plans, California’s Strategic Highway Safety Plan (SHSP), the National Road Safety 
Strategy, and current best practices in safety including the Safe System Approach. The team will 
confirm a vision statement and goals that go beyond engineering to encompass the four (4) E’s 
of Safety. The vision statement will guide the City’s approach to improving roadway safety.  
This task will happen in conjunction with Task 4 and Task 5 to allow for the crash and roadway 
data analysis to inform the development of goals and objectives that directly speak to crash 
patterns, trends, and risk factors within the City. The goals and objectives are created to 
supplement the vision and mission statements of the City and are aimed at measurable actions 
the City can take to improve roadway safety performance. The LRSP goals and objectives will 
include identifying emphasis crash types and patterns to be addressed, crash risk factors, 
identifying high-priority locations for crash risk, and implementing the low-cost countermeasures 
systemically during regular maintenance or capital improvement projects to reduce fatal and 
severe injury crashes. This will be virtually presented to the Sustainability and Infrastructure 
Commission to request feedback and recommendations for the goal-setting process.  
Kittelson will work with the City to collect and synthesize recent relevant City safety-related 
plans, engineering studies, and safety improvements. This information will be referenced in 
subsequent tasks and documented in the final LRSP. Comments will be collected and 
incorporated into the final LRSP as a part of Task 7. 
Task 3 Deliverables  
▪ 
Memorandum summarizing vision statement, mission statement, goals, and priorities  
▪ 
Virtual presentation, materials, and notes to the Sustainability and Infrastructure 
Commission 
 
TASK 4. COLLISION AND ROADWAY ANALYSIS  
 
TASK 4 PURPOSE  
Through a systemic approach, Kittelson will identify roadway characteristics and user behaviors 
associated with crash risk based on crash history (“risk factors”) to prioritize locations based on 
the presence of risk factors rather than solely focusing on where crashes have occurred. 
Task 4.1 Comprehensive Database Establishment 
Kittelson will obtain SWITRS and TIMS crash data for the latest five years of available data. 
Kittelson will work with the City to determine any additional crash data available and confirm 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 09487D47-CA60-4581-A592-4AB64F1F522B
328 of 615

--- page 329 ---
Signing In Process
the desired data period. A consolidated, uniform set of reported crashes will be mapped in a 
GIS-based platform. Kittelson will also obtain available land use and roadway data to help 
identify any contributing factors related to crashes in subsequent tasks.  
Task 4.2 Crash Trends and Patterns Safety Analysis 
With the roadway data and crash databases established in Task 4.1, Kittelson will conduct a 
network screening to identify high crash frequency and/or severity locations for both 
intersections and roadway segments. Kittelson will prepare a highly graphical memorandum of 
analysis findings, which will include a list of priority locations, identified risk factors, and common 
contributing factors for the crashes. The memorandum will include charts and exhibits illustrating 
key findings related to factors like personal attributes/human factors (e.g., driver impairment, 
behavior), vehicular and equipment factors (e.g., braking, occupant restraints), and roadway 
and environmental factors (e.g., speed limits, roadway geometric design).  
As an example, we have constructed a crash tree diagram presented in Figure 1 indicating the 
breakdown of crashes by party type (i.e., vehicles, pedestrians, bicyclists, motorcyclists) and 
location (e.g., intersections vs. roadways). Within the project, such findings could be further 
disaggregated by crash type, for example, to focus desired emphasis areas. The tree 
demonstrates, for example, the elevated risk for pedestrians along roadways – they are involved 
in 7% of reported crashes but 45% of fatal and severe injury crashes. 
 
Figure 1: Crash distribution by Mode 
Task 4 Deliverables  
▪ 
Consolidated crash and roadway databases in GIS format  
▪ 
Memorandum summarizing crash patterns and trends 
 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 09487D47-CA60-4581-A592-4AB64F1F522B
329 of 615

--- page 330 ---
Signing In Process
TASK 5. EMPHASIS AREAS AND COUNTERMEASURES  
 
TASK 5 PURPOSE  
Establish emphasis areas for the City. These will be informed by findings from prior tasks and input 
from the City, stakeholders, and public to ensure sufficient buy-in for future improvements. 
Identify countermeasures to address emphasis areas. 
Task 5.1 Identify Emphasis Areas 
Kittelson will identify emphasis areas using results from the safety analysis in Task 4. Each emphasis 
area will include goals and strategies that will support the LRSP vision and reduction in crashes 
consistent with the emphasis area. Kittelson will also help identify safety partners that will be 
instrumental in supporting the goals of each emphasis area and the vision of the LRSP as a 
whole. Kittelson will prepare a memorandum that includes the emphasis areas and their goals 
and strategies and supporting safety partners.  
Task 5.2 Identify Countermeasures 
Kittelson will work with City of San Mateo staff to identify strategies to help achieve the safety 
vision established as part of Task 3 and address findings of Task 4. Engineering and non-
engineering countermeasures will be considered when identifying potential solutions. Kittelson 
will develop a memorandum that provides a countermeasures toolbox applicable to their safety 
crash trends. The toolbox will include:  
Engineering Countermeasures most relevant to the roadway safety vision and goals, high-priority 
locations, and crash risk factors using the latest version of the Caltrans Local Roadway Safety 
Manual, the FHWA’s Crash Modification (CMF) Clearinghouse website, HSIP set-aside categories, 
and other recent countermeasures used or encouraged by the City.  
Non-Engineering Countermeasures including education, equitable enforcement, and 
emergency services strategies that can be implemented to modify user behavior or reduce the 
likelihood of severe crash outcomes. Partnership with the stakeholder team, review of NHTSA’s 
Countermeasures that work, the Caltrans SHSP Implementation Plan, and Safe Systems 
approach and its five elements will help identify feasible solutions that can be utilized to target 
behavior-related crash factors identified in Task 4.  
The emphasis areas, strategies and the countermeasure toolbox will be virtually presented to the 
Sustainability and Infrastructure Commission to request feedback and recommendations on the 
emphasis areas and countermeasures. Kittelson will produce a draft memorandum and will 
revise based on a consolidated set of comments from City staff and additional stakeholders 
identified by the City. 
Task 5 Deliverables  
▪ 
Memorandum summarizing emphasis areas and strategies to increase safety 
performance pertinent to each of the emphasis areas  
▪ 
Countermeasures toolbox memorandum  
▪ 
Virtual presentation, materials, and notes to the Sustainability and Infrastructure 
Commission 
 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 09487D47-CA60-4581-A592-4AB64F1F522B
330 of 615

--- page 331 ---
Signing In Process
TASK 6. PROJECT IDENTIFICATION, EVALUATION, AND 
PRIORITIZATION  
 
TASK 6 PURPOSE  
Identify high priority locations which will be used as the basis for assessing safety improvement 
opportunities and for future safety evaluations by the City. Prepare an actionable 
implementation plan for the City LRSP. 
Task 6.1 Identify Priority Locations 
Kittelson will identify up to 20 priority locations based on results from Tasks 4 and 5. Kittelson will 
start with the top high crash frequency and/or severity locations identified in Task 4 and will 
consult with the City to identify any changes to the top 20, based on recently completed or 
planned improvements. Kittelson will summarize the priority locations and include comparative 
attributes with the City, which could include, for example:  
• 
Location type (intersection, roadway, or all)  
• 
Crash history (number of crashes, crash severity score, targeted crashes)  
• 
Top reported crash types and primary crash factors  
• 
Alignment with emphasis areas 
Task 6.2 Identify Potential Improvements 
Kittelson will identify potential improvements using the list of priority locations from Task 6.1. 
Kittelson will develop systemic and location-specific project concepts for up to ten (10) locations 
based on the analysis in Task 4 and the countermeasure toolbox developed in Task 5.2. Project 
development will consist of countermeasures matched to locations based on crash history and 
systemic location characteristics.  
Task 6.3 Prepare Implementation Plan 
Kittelson will work with City of San Mateo and key stakeholders to develop an actionable LRSP 
implementation plan to prioritize projects, identify funding opportunities to implement projects, 
and what actions are needed to achieve the goals, objectives, and vision set forth in the LRSP. 
The implementation will also serve as a resource to identify and engage key stakeholders and 
partner agencies to maintain momentum and commitment to the LRSP mission statement and 
help address cross-jurisdictional roadway safety performance.  
A first component of the implementation plan will be developing the communication approach 
to keep City staff, key stakeholders (i.e., PDT members), and multidisciplinary partners engaged 
with the LRSP implementation. This could take a variety of forms from developing an approach 
for annual reporting on safety performance to forming a City safety steering committee to meet, 
discuss, and move forward safety strategies for implementation. We will outline the best 
approaches and strategies for the City to keep everyone informed and actively involved as part 
of LRSP implementation. As part of this plan, Kittelson will also help the city to develop 
approaches and practices for integrating safety management into existing work practices.  
Our team will prepare a draft LRSP implementation plan providing guidance and actions for City 
staff to implement the LRSP vision. Based on a consolidated set of comments provided by San 
Mateo staff and the PDT, Kittelson will finalize the LRSP implementation plan for inclusion in the 
overall LRSP.  
DocuSign Envelope ID: 09487D47-CA60-4581-A592-4AB64F1F522B

Signing In Process
Task 6.4 HSIP Applications 
Kittelson will work with City staff to identify and prepare up to two (2) HSIP grant applications for 
the HSIP Cycle 12. Kittelson will prepare materials necessary for the applications, including 
project concepts, cost estimates, and maps, and provide all supporting documentation and 
materials.  
Task 6 Deliverables  
▪ 
Priority Locations List (up to 20 locations)  
▪ 
Up to ten (10) project concepts  
▪ 
Draft/Final LRSP implementation plan  
▪ 
Two HSIP grant applications, including preliminary concept designs, cost estimates, and 
supporting information necessary for the applications. 
 
TASK 7. DRAFT AND FINAL LOCAL ROAD SAFETY PLAN 
 
TASK 7 PURPOSE  
Prepare an LRSP including goals, emphasis areas, high-priority locations, improvements, and 
strategies to implement roadway safety management process, and to improve roadway safety 
in the City of San Mateo. 
Kittelson will prepare a draft and final City of San Mateo Local Roadway Safety Plan following 
the Local Roadway Safety Plan guidance and requirements. The LRSP will include:  
• 
Executive Summary  
• 
Previous efforts to address roadway safety  
• 
Partners and process to develop the LRSP  
• 
Vision and mission statement, goals, and objectives of the LRSP  
• 
Current roadway safety performance including the high-injury network and recurring 
crash trends and patterns, crash data modeling and risk analysis results  
• 
Emphasis areas  
• 
A list of high-priority locations for future City evaluations  
• 
Safety countermeasures and strategies identified in the Four Safety E’s section of the LRSP  
• 
Project concepts for five (5) systemic safety project templates  
• 
Implementation plan for LRSP, which includes the funding sources, key stakeholders’ 
continuous involvement, and suggested timeline for updating the LRSP 
 
Our team will prepare a draft LRSP report and distribute it to the City of San Mateo staff for 
review. 
We will revise the draft based on comments compiled and provided by City staff, City Council, 
and partners. We will provide all textual work products in both PDF and Microsoft Word formats, 
as desired. All relevant project data will be packaged and shared to the City for their future use.  
Task 7 Deliverables  
▪ 
Draft LRSP  
▪ 
Final LRSP 
DocuSign Envelope ID: 09487D47-CA60-4581-A592-4AB64F1F522B
332 of 615

--- page 333 ---
Signing In Process
TASK 8. ENVIRONMENTAL DOCUMENT  
 
TASK 8 PURPOSE  
Prepare a guidance document that identifies potential safety impacts and issues for new 
projects which will trigger a detailed environmental analysis. 
Kittelson will prepare a guidance document that outlines the process to identify issues which will 
trigger a detailed environmental analysis for priority projects. Kittelson will review and follow the 
process outlined in Caltrans Interim Land Development and Intergovernmental Review (LDIGR) 
Safety Review Practitioners Guide, published in 2020, to develop guidance for safety impacts 
review. The guidance document will include the following, but not limited to:  
▪ 
Safety plans and programs relevant to specific areas/throughout the City  
▪ 
Map that includes high-injury network, and/or network-screening results from Task 4 that 
refer to the underlying crash risk presence on City’s roadways 
The LRSP can help the City of San Mateo obtain resources and funding to improve safety 
performance on roadways and will also be an input to assessing the potential safety impacts of 
new land use projects and plans. This environmental guidance document will help the City 
conduct its own safety review in the future to determine whether potential projects’ contribution 
to the adverse safety impacts identified through the process and maps above constitutes a 
significant impact under California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA).  
We will revise the draft based on one round of consolidated comments provided by City staff, 
City Council, and partners. We will provide all textual work products in both PDF and Microsoft 
Word formats, as desired.  
OPTIONAL Task 8.1 Scope and Scale of CEQA Analysis 
Given that the scope and scale of the safety projects are unknown at this time, Kittelson will work 
with City staff to determine the scope and scale of CEQA analysis required to environmentally 
clear the priority projects identified in the LRSP. Following that determination, Kittelson will work 
with the City to prepare a detailed scope and budget for this optional task to conduct the work. 
No budget has been assumed for this task.  
Task 8 Deliverables  
▪ 
Draft Environmental Guidance Document  
▪ 
Final Environmental Guidance Document

EXHIBIT C 
 
INSURANCE REQUIREMENTS 
 
MINIMUM SCOPE OF INSURANCE  
Coverage shall be at least as broad as: 
 
1. Commercial General Liability (CGL):  Insurance Services Office (ISO) Form CG 00 01 12 07 
covering CGL on an “occurrence” basis, including products-completed operations, personal & 
advertising injury, with limits no less than $2,000,000 per occurrence. If a general aggregate 
limit applies, either the general aggregate limit shall apply separately to this project/location or 
the general aggregate limit shall be twice the required occurrence limit. 
 
2. Automobile Liability:  ISO Form Number CA 00 01 covering any auto (Code 1), or if 
CONSULTANT has no owned autos, hired, (Code 8) and non-owned autos (Code 9), with limit no 
less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily injury and property damage. 
 
3. Workers’ Compensation: as required by the State of California, with Statutory Limits, and 
Employer’s Liability Insurance with limit of no less than $1,000,000 per accident for bodily 
injury or disease.  
 
4. Professional Liability (Errors and Omissions): Insurance appropriate to the CONSULTANT’s 
profession, with limit no less than $1,000,000 per occurrence or claim, $2,000,000 aggregate 
 
If the CONSULTANT maintains higher limits than the minimums shown above, the City requires and 
shall be entitled to coverage for the higher limits maintained by the CONSULTANT. 
 
Other Insurance Provisions 
The insurance policies are to contain, or be endorsed to contain, the following provisions: 
 
Additional Insured Status 
The City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents are to be covered as insureds on 
the auto policy for liability arising out of automobiles owned, leased, hired or borrowed by or on behalf 
of the CONSULTANT; and on the CGL policy with respect to liability arising out of work or operations 
performed by or on behalf of the CONSULTANT including materials, parts or equipment furnished in 
connection with such work or operations. General liability coverage can be provided in the form of an 
endorsement to the CONSULTANT’s insurance (at least as broad as ISO Form CG 20 10, 11 85 or both 
CG 20 10 and CG 20 37 forms if later revisions used). 
 
Primary Coverage 
For any claims related to this contract, the CONSULTANT’s insurance coverage shall be primary 
insurance as respects the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, and agents. Any 
insurance or self-insurance maintained by the City, its elected and appointed officials, employees, or 
agents shall be excess of the CONSULTANT’s insurance and shall not contribute with it.

Signing In Process
Professional Services  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Page 11 of 11 
Rev 5/22 
Notice of Cancellation 
Each insurance policy required above shall provide that coverage shall not be canceled, except after 
thirty (30) days’ prior written notice (10 days for non-payment) has been given to the City. 
 
Waiver of Subrogation 
CONSULTANT hereby grants to City a waiver of any right to subrogation which any insurer of said 
CONSULTANT may acquire against the City by virtue of the payment of any loss under such insurance.  
CONSULTANT agrees to obtain any endorsement that may be necessary to effect this waiver of 
subrogation, but this provision applies regardless of whether or not the City has received a waiver of 
subrogation endorsement from the insurer.   
 
Deductibles and Self-Insured Retentions 
Any deductibles or self-insured retentions must be declared to and approved by the City. The City may 
require the CONSULTANT to purchase coverage with a lower deductible or retention or provide proof 
of ability to pay losses and related investigations, claim administration, and defense expenses within 
the retention.   
 
Acceptability of Insurers 
Insurance is to be placed with insurers with a current A.M. Best’s rating of no less than A:VII, unless 
otherwise acceptable to the City. 
 
Verification of Coverage 
CONSULTANT shall furnish the City with original certificates and amendatory endorsements or copies 
of the applicable policy language effecting coverage required by this clause.  All certificates and 
endorsements are to be received and approved by the City before work commences.  However, failure 
to obtain the required documents prior to the work beginning shall not waive the CONSULTANT’s 
obligation to provide them.  The City reserves the right to require complete, certified copies of all 
required insurance policies, including endorsements required by these specifications, at any time.

SUBJECT:
Rail Shuttle Bus Service Administration – Agreements
RECOMMENDATION:
Approve the Amended and Restated Rail Shuttle Bus Service Administration Agreements for the Campus Drive and Norfolk 
Shuttles with Commute.org for extension of shuttle operations for a total of $73,375 and authorize the Public Works 
Director to execute the agreement in substantially the form presented.
BACKGROUND:
On August 18, 2014, City Council approved agreements with the Peninsula Corridor Joint Powers Board (PCJPB) for shuttle 
bus service on two routes (Campus Drive and Norfolk Area) that connect the City’s Caltrain Stations with the major 
business parks. The agreements have been extended by amendment, with the most recent Amendment 7 extending the 
term of service through June 30, 2022. Beginning in July 2022, the shuttle program administration will be managed by 
Commute.org, requiring an amended agreement outlining the new management structure. For both the initial agreement 
with the PCJPB and the new management under Commute.org, the City has agreed to fund 25 percent of costs. 
The most recent amendment was approved by City Council on June 21, 2021, extending the contract from July 1, 2021 
until June 30, 2022. The proposed amended and restated agreements will extend the contract through June 30, 2023, 
update the management and operations structure for the shuttle program, and update the mutual funding responsibilities 
for Commute.org and the City. Under the new agreement, the City will continue to contribute 25 percent of the program 
costs, or approximately $73,375 annually.
Due to service disruptions as a result of the COVID-19 pandemic and related local health orders and the reduced number 
of commuters utilizing shuttle service in favor of telecommuting, the Norfolk shuttle route is currently on hold. The route 
and schedule for the Norfolk route will be provided by Commute.org when the service resumes.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The total cost for shuttle operations is $293,500. Commute.org’s maximum contribution is 75 percent of the total cost, or 
$220,125. The City is responsible for the remaining portion of the total cost, including any amounts over the estimates 
shown in the table below. This equates to approximately 25 percent of the total cost, or $73,375. This amount is $3,675 
more than the prior fiscal year. The City’s portions will be funded from the Citywide Parking and Transportation budget. 
The table on the following page summarizes the estimated costs and funding sources for the shuttles.

(25.0%) of the total actual annual costs of operating the Shuttle Service described in Section 1 of 
this Amended and Restated Agreement.  Lead Organization's 25.0% share shall equal Thirty-
Eight Thousand One Hundred Fifty dollars ($38,150) annually, subject to a final adjustment as 
provided below.  
Lead Organization shall pay based on monthly invoices from Commute.org.  Lead 
Organization shall pay each invoice within thirty (30) days from the date of the invoice.  In the 
event 25.0% of the actual cost of operating the Shuttle during the term of this Amended and 
Restated Agreement exceeds Lead Organization's total payments, excluding any Administration 
fee, upon adequate substantiation of such cost overrun, Lead Organization shall pay to 
Commute.org the difference within thirty (30) days of Commute.org's request for it.  All 
payments required under this Amended and Restated Agreement shall be made by check or

EXHIBIT C
MARKETING PROGRAM
(As of July 1, 2022)
Commute.org has developed a countywide shuttle marketing plan that serves as the basis for 
promoting the shuttle program throughout San Mateo County. Commute.org and its partners use 
a variety of tools to create and execute focused marketing for the shuttle program.
The Commute.org and SamTrans websites serve as a primary source of information about the 
shuttle program. Riders, and potential riders, use the sites to get information about routes and 
schedules as well as real-time vehicle location tracking. The mobile version of the Commute.org 
site is optimized to provide shuttle riders with streamlined access to the information they need 
during their commute.
Commute.org maintains and publishes route specific GTFS data that is used by transit planning 
apps including those managed by Google, Apple, 511 and app developers. Passengers can plan 
trips that include shuttles anywhere Commute.org shuttles operate. Route and schedule 
information is also available in hard-copy format.
Commute.org works with the cities and property owners where shuttles operate to install and 
maintain signage indicating where the shuttles stop. New signage is being installed at stop 
locations throughout San Mateo County. 
Commute.org shuttle and employer outreach staff provide presentations about the shuttle 
program directly to employers and their employees as well as at community events. Rider 
appreciation events are conducted throughout the year and allow the Commute.org team to thank 
shuttle riders for their commitment to using shuttles for first/last mile transportation. 
352 of 615

--- page 353 ---
Att 1 - Campus Drive Amended and Restated Agreement.pdf
14
18275263.3 
EXHIBIT D
FUNDING BENCHMARKS
As of July 1, 2022
Following are funding benchmarks that will be evaluated for the Shuttle Service to remain 
eligible for continued grant funding.  Benchmarks that are not met may result, in Commute.org’s 
sole discretion, in the elimination of funding.  Due to the competitive grant process, a Shuttle 
Service meeting the following benchmarks is not guaranteed to receive continued funding:
1. Cost per passenger equal to or less than $9.00;
2. Passengers per service hour equal to or greater than 15.
THE BALANCE OF THIS PAGE IS INTENTIONALLY LEFT BLANK.

Twenty-Five Percent (25.0%) financial share annually, excluding contributions from 
Commute.org described under Section 5 of the Agreement, Maximum Contribution; Payment; as 
amended.
Lead Organization may seek appropriate contributions from participating organizations 
for reimbursement of amounts expended by Lead Organization hereunder, it being understood 
that Lead Organization shall not seek or make a profit from serving as Lead Organization.  
Commute.org has no responsibility to collect any such amounts from such other organizations 
participating in the Program to be serviced by the Shuttle.

responsibilities for the Shuttle. 
Commute.org shall be responsible for supplying and/or completing any grant-related 
reporting information.  Lead Organization shall assist Commute.org in obtaining appropriate 
reporting information.

by either party without cause by giving thirty (30) days prior written notice to the other in the 
manner provided below.  
All subsidy funds provided for the Program shall be generated from non-Commute.org

EXHIBIT C
MARKETING PROGRAM
(As of July 1, 2022)
Commute.org has developed a countywide shuttle marketing plan that serves as the basis for 
promoting the shuttle program throughout San Mateo County. Commute.org and its partners use 
a variety of tools to create and execute focused marketing for the shuttle program.
The Commute.org and SamTrans websites serve as a primary source of information about the 
shuttle program. Riders, and potential riders, use the sites to get information about routes and 
schedules as well as real-time vehicle location tracking. The mobile version of the Commute.org 
site is optimized to provide shuttle riders with streamlined access to the information they need 
during their commute.
Commute.org maintains and publishes route specific GTFS data that is used by transit planning 
apps including those managed by Google, Apple, 511 and app developers. Passengers can plan 
trips that include shuttles anywhere Commute.org shuttles operate. Route and schedule 
information is also available in hard-copy format.
Commute.org works with the cities and property owners where shuttles operate to install and 
maintain signage indicating where the shuttles stop. New signage is being installed at stop 
locations throughout San Mateo County. 
Commute.org shuttle and employer outreach staff provide presentations about the shuttle 
program directly to employers and their employees as well as at community events. Rider 
appreciation events are conducted throughout the year and allow the Commute.org team to thank 
shuttle riders for their commitment to using shuttles for first/last mile transportation.

--- page 370 ---
Att 2 - Norfolk Amended and Restated Agreement.pdf

EXHIBIT D
FUNDING BENCHMARKS
(As of July 1, 2022)
  
Following are funding benchmarks that will be evaluated for the Shuttle Service to remain 
eligible for continued grant funding.  Benchmarks that are not met may result, in Commute.org’s 
sole discretion, in the elimination of funding.  Due to the competitive grant process, a Shuttle 
Service meeting the following benchmarks is not guaranteed to receive continued funding:
1. Cost per passenger equal to or less than $9.00;
2. Passengers per service hour equal to or greater than 15.

CITY OF SAN MATEO
CITY OF SAN MATEO
Agenda Report
City Hall
330 W. 20th Avenue
San Mateo CA 94403
www.cityofsanmateo.org
Agenda Number:  19
Section Name: CONSENT CALENDAR Account Number: 72-4674-09
File ID: 22-6436
TO:
City Council
FROM:
Drew Corbett, City Manager
PREPARED BY:
Public Works Department
MEETING DATE:
July 18, 2022 
SUBJECT:
SF-Peninsula Regional Purewater Project – Memorandum of Agreement
RECOMMENDATION:
Adopt a Resolution to approve a Memorandum of Agreement between the Bay Area Water Supply and Conservation 
Agency, California Water Service Company, City and County of San Francisco through its Public Utilities Commission, Mid-
Peninsula Water District, and Silicon Valley Clean Water for the development of a basis of design report for the SF-
Peninsula Regional Purewater project, which will incorporate the City as a party to the agreement and commit to a cost 
share not to exceed $100,000; and authorize the City Manager to execute the agreement in substantially the form 
presented. 
BACKGROUND:
With on-going drought conditions in the area and a growing population, there is a regional need to explore alternative 
water supply sources for improved resiliency. Since 2016, the City has been working with the Bay Area Water Supply and 
Conservation Agency, California Water Service Company, City and County of San Francisco through its Public Utilities 
Commission (SFPUC), and Silicon Valley Clean Water (SVCW) to study potable water reuse opportunities in the Mid-
Peninsula region. The City entered into a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) with these agencies and a number of 
assessments were developed as part of the Potable Reuse Exploratory Plan (PREP) effort. 

PREP Phase 1 Study:  Finalized in 2017, this effort evaluated groundwater reuse replenishment in the San Mateo 
Plain Basin and reservoir water augmentation at the Crystal Springs Reservoir and Bear Gulch Reservoir, potential 
water supply options from wastewater treated by SVCW and the City’s wastewater treatment plant, and 
alternative locations for advanced water purification treatment facilities. 

PREP Phase 2 Study: This study evaluated options and determined construction costs for an advanced treatment 
water purification facility and pipelines. This work was finalized in 2019 and highlighted the need for additional 
analyses to evaluate the impacts of reservoir water augmentation to Crystal Springs Reservoir. Further, the 
participating agencies identified an opportunity to explore direct potable reuse options in addition to the 
development of an indirect approach. 

PREP Phase 3 Feasibility Study: Mixing, retention times, and operations at the Crystal Springs Reservoir were 
assessed and alternatives for direct potable reuse on the San Francisco Mid-Peninsula region were evaluated. 
Alternatives, costs, benefits, and risks were developed for several options. 
Through Phase 3 of the PREP, all parties have supported the recommendation to proceed with the SF-Peninsula Regional 
Purewater (SPRP) effort, which would involve the development of preliminary engineering design drawings for an 
advanced water purification facility that would treat wastewater generated from the City and SVCW. This facility would 
produce water suitable for indirect potable reuse and reservoir water augmentation and supply SFPUC’s Crystal Springs 
Reservoir. The facility would also be designed to allow for a future expansion for direct potable reuse / treated drinking 
375 of 615

--- page 376 ---
CITY OF SAN MATEO
water augmentation for Redwood City, Cal Water, and possibly the Mid-Peninsula Water District. The project would 
produce up to 12 million gallons per day of potable water and is estimated to cost $700 million. The development of the 
SPRP project preliminary design documents and CEQA checklist costs approximately $1 million and the requested 
contribution from the City for this phase is $92,700. However, staff recommends for the City Council to approve a 
contribution in an amount up to $100,000 to account for any unforeseen costs. 
While the City is not a water purveyor, the effluent from the wastewater treatment plant could be a valuable resource to 
the region in addressing extended years of drought. Through a regional approach and collaboration with partnering 
agencies, the City would continue to support an integrated water management strategy. Staff recommends approval of a 
Memorandum of Agreement for the SPRP project.
BUDGET IMPACT:
The City's contribution to the total cost of SPRP project of $1,030,000 will be capped at $100,000 and will be funded from 
the Sewer Fund operating budget - Sewer Fund Engineering Services/Clean Water Program.
ENVIRONMENTAL DETERMINATION:
This is not a project subject to CEQA, because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not result in direct 
or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 15378(b)(5).)
NOTICE PROVIDED
All meeting noticing requirements were met.

Azalea Mitch, Director of Public Works
amitch@cityofsanmateo.org
(650) 522-7303

CITY OF SAN MATEO
RESOLUTION NO. ____ (2022)
APPROVE A MEMORANDUM OF AGREEMENT FOR THE SF-PENISULA REGIONAL PUREWATER PROJECT BASIS 
OF DESIGN REPORT
WHEREAS, the City supports environmental responsibility and recognizes the need to analyze the 
potential to produce drinking water from the City’s wastewater; and
WHEREAS, the City supports local, regional, and State goals for recycled water use; and
WHEREAS, since 2016, the City has been working with the Bay Area Water Supply and Conservation 
Agency, California Water Service Company, City and County of San Francisco’s Public Utilities Commission, and 
Silicon Valley Water (collectively “Agencies”) to study potential potable water reuse opportunities in the mid-
peninsula region; and
WHEREAS, the City entered into a Memorandum of Understanding with the Agencies for a Potable 
Reuse Exploratory Plan (PREP) effort; and
WHEREAS, through PREP, the City and Agencies support a recommendation to proceed further in 
development of the SF-Peninsula Regional Purewater Project (SPRP); and
WHEREAS, the development of a basis of design report is necessary component to proceed with 
development of SPRP; and 
WHEREAS, the basis of design report is estimated to cost approximately $1,030,000, with a contribution 
from the City in an amount not to exceed $100,000; and
WHEREAS, the City desires to proceed in the development of the basis of design report for the SPRP.
NOW, THEREFORE, THE CITY COUNCIL OF THE CITY OF SAN MATEO, CALIFORNIA, HEREBY RESOLVES 
that:
1.
This City Council action in approving a Memorandum of Agreement with the Agencies is not a 
project subject to CEQA review because it is an organizational or administrative activity that will not 
result in direct or indirect physical changes in the environment. (CEQA Guidelines Section 
15378(b)(5).)
2.
The City Manager is authorized to execute the Memorandum of Agreement between the Bay Area 
Water Supply and Conservation Agency, California Water Service Company, City and County of San 
Francisco through the Public Utilities Commission, Mid-Peninsula Water District, and Silicon Valley 
Clean Water to participate in the development of Basis of Design Report for the SF-Peninsula 
Regional Purewater Project and commit the City to a cost share not to exceed $100,000.
